EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strongly forced band of convection developed over West Virginia in the morning ahead of an approaching front, and quickly lifted north-northeastward into Pennsylvania through the early afternoon. Despite relatively modest instability, very strong deep layer shear, along with largely unidirectional southerly flow and elongated hodographs, supported the continuation of significant severe storms. Large impact was felt from straight-line wind, with gusts in the 60 to 80 MPH range, locally higher, causing widespread damage to trees, power lines, and structures. The line of storms continued into Pennsylvania, where the environment was more favorable for tornadic development.  In addition to damaging winds, a number of large hail reports were received, with some stones as large as 1.75 inch diameter. Also, embedded supercells/mesovortices produced five confirmed tornadoes during the afternoon (one of which crossed from Fayette County into Westmoreland County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were downed, blocking Route 56 between Old Mission Road and PA-954.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As low pressure intensified over Upstate New York early on January 1st it pulled cold air in behind it, allowing rain to transition to snow. This initial snow behind the low pressure resulted in several inches of wet snow accumulation across the higher elevations in southern Erie County and northern Crawford County. This low pressure exited off the New England coast during the afternoon as cold air continued to flow in on westerly winds, allowing snow to transition to lake effect bands that continued into January 2nd. These lake effect bands were heavier, with rates peaking at greater than 1 inch per hour on January 2nd, before activity lost organization and began shifting northeast and out of the area by the evening on January 2nd. The one-two combination of snow behind the low pressure and lake effect allowed totals to add up across parts of Northwest Pennsylvania. A trained spotter in Greene Township measured 19.3 inches of snow with this event, with 17 inches near Edinboro, 14 inches in Venango Township, and 9.6 inches at Erie International Airport. During the heaviest snow, low visibility and snow-covered roads led to difficult travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As low pressure intensified over Upstate New York early on January 1st it pulled cold air in behind it, allowing rain to transition to snow. This initial snow behind the low pressure resulted in several inches of wet snow accumulation across the higher elevations in southern Erie County. This low pressure exited off the New England coast during the afternoon as cold air continued to flow in on westerly winds, allowing snow to transition to lake effect bands that continued into January 2nd. These lake effect bands were heavier, with rates peaking at greater than 1 inch per hour on January 2nd, before activity lost organization and began shifting northeast and out of the area by the evening on January 2nd. A trained spotter in Greene Township measured 19.3 inches of snow with this event. Other selected spotter reports include 17 inches near Edinboro, 15.2 inches in Union City, and 14.7 inches in Greenfield Township. A CoCoRaHS observer in Corry measured 12 inches of snow. During the heaviest snow, low visibility and snow-covered roads led to difficult travel conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface trough developed over the Great Lakes on February 20th and lingered into February 21st before surface high pressure worked into the region. The trough focused cold, west-northwest flow across the partially unfrozen waters of Lake Erie, allowing for lake effect snow into Northwest Pennsylvania. This snow began organizing early on the 20th with peak rates during the day and through the evening on the 20th, when snow rates of up to 1 inch per hour occurred. With temperatures in the 10s to low 20s, this snow led to reduced visibility as low as one-quarter of a mile and snow-covered roads. Accidents were reported, with I-90 closed for a period of time during the morning of the 20th near Girard due to a multi-car accident. Snow gradually lessened in intensity overnight into the morning of the 21st. A peak snowfall of 11.5 inches was reported by a trained spotter in Edinboro, with 11 inches also reported in Greenfield and West Springfield Townships. 9.5 inches was reported in Millcreek, with 8 inches in Harborcreek, 7.8 inches in North East, 7.5 inches Northwest Harborcreek, and 6.8 inches in Erie.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A surface trough developed over the Great Lakes on February 20th and lingered into February 21st before surface high pressure worked into the region. The trough focused cold, west-northwest flow across the partially unfrozen waters of Lake Erie, allowing for lake effect snow into Northwest Pennsylvania. This snow began organizing early on the 20th with peak rates during the day and through the evening on the 20th, when snow rates of up to 1 inch per hour occurred. With temperatures in the 10s to low 20s, this snow led to reduced visibility as low as one-quarter of a mile and snow-covered roads. Accidents were reported. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Behind a cold front that crossed early in the morning on January 3, 2025, west-northwest winds ushered in colder air across Lake Erie, setting up another round of lake effect snow showers and squalls across Northwest Pennsylvania. The lake effect activity was most intense from late morning through the evening on January 3rd, with activity gradually winding down on January 4th and tapering to flurries or light snow showers January 4th into January 5th. Event total accumulations reached heavy snow criteria across inland Northwest Pennsylvania, which along with wind gusts of 20 to 30 MPH led to a prolonged period of poor travel conditions due to periods of reduced visibility and snow-covered roads. A CoCoRaHS observer in Corry recorded 10.9 inches of snow, with 9.5 inches measured by a spotter in Edinboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As low pressure intensified over Upstate New York early on January 1st it pulled cold air in behind it, allowing rain to transition to snow. This initial snow behind the low pressure resulted in several inches of wet snow accumulation across the higher elevations in southern Erie County and northern Crawford County. This low pressure exited off the New England coast during the afternoon as cold air continued to flow in on westerly winds, allowing snow to transition to lake effect bands that continued into January 2nd. These lake effect bands were heavier, with rates peaking at greater than 1 inch per hour on January 2nd, before activity lost organization and began shifting northeast and out of the area by the evening on January 2nd. The one-two combination of snow behind the low pressure and lake effect allowed totals to add up across parts of Northwest Pennsylvania. A trained spotter in Greene Township measured 19.3 inches of snow with this event, with 17 inches near Edinboro, 14 inches in Venango Township, and 9.6 inches at Erie International Airport. During the heaviest snow, low visibility and snow-covered roads led to difficult travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As low pressure intensified over Upstate New York early on January 1st it pulled cold air in behind it, allowing rain to transition to snow. This initial snow behind the low pressure resulted in only light accumulations across northern Erie County. This low pressure exited off the New England coast during the afternoon as cold air continued to flow in on westerly winds, allowing snow to transition to lake effect bands that continued into January 2nd. These lake effect bands were heavier, with rates peaking at around 1 inch per hour on January 2nd, before activity lost organization and began shifting northeast and out of the area by the evening on January 2nd. The lake effect snow produced heavier accumulations across northern Erie County. Erie International Airport reported 9.6 inches of snow with the lake effect, with a trained spotter in Millcreek Township measuring 5.8 inches. During the heaviest snow, low visibility and snow-covered roads led to difficult travel conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deepening low pressure tracked into the upper Ohio Valley the morning of February 16th, before the low pressure transferred to off the east coast that afternoon and evening. This allowed for a period of rain changing to accumulating wet snow as the low pressure lifted into the Ohio Valley, followed by continuing lake effect snow as cold, west-northwest winds wrapped in behind the low into early February 17th. An initial period of impactful snow accumulation occurred from late morning into the afternoon on the 16th, as rain initially changed to a period of steady to heavy snow with the low pressure. Periods of moderate to heavy lake effect snow showers and squalls were most prominent during the evening and overnight hours on the 16th into early on the 17th, before winding down and exiting to the east into the 17th. Wind gusts to 30 MPH on the 16th led to some blowing snow, reducing visibility and making it harder for road crews to keep roads clear. Periods of reduced visibility and snow-covered, icy roads led to a prolonged period of difficult travel conditions across Northwest Pennsylvania. A peak snowfall of 9 inches was measured by a trained spotter in Venango Township. Other select reports include 8.8 inches in West Springfield Township, 7.8 inches in Meadville, and 7.5 inches in Corry.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of surface low pressure was draped west to east across the southern Great Lakes January 14th and 15th, with cold west-northwest winds wrapping around this trough and over Lake Erie. This set the stage for a period of moderate to heavy lake effect snow into parts of Northwest PA. The heaviest accumulations occurred during the afternoon and evening hours of the 14th, when a west to east oriented band of snow pushed off of Lake Erie with snow rates briefly in excess of one inch per hour. Snow continued through January 15th, though gradually lessened in intensity. Select trained spotter snowfall reports include 15 inches in Greene Township, 14 inches in Union City and Millcreek, 13 inches in North East and 12 inches in Edinboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface trough developed over the Great Lakes on February 20th and lingered into February 21st before surface high pressure worked into the region. The trough focused cold, west-northwest flow across the partially unfrozen waters of Lake Erie, allowing for lake effect snow into Northwest Pennsylvania. This snow began organizing early on the 20th with peak rates during the day and through the evening on the 20th, when snow rates of up to 1 inch per hour occurred. With temperatures in the 10s to low 20s, this snow led to reduced visibility as low as one-quarter of a mile and snow-covered roads. Accidents were reported, with I-90 closed for a period of time during the morning of the 20th near Girard due to a multi-car accident. Snow gradually lessened in intensity overnight into the morning of the 21st. A peak snowfall of 11.5 inches was reported by a trained spotter in Edinboro, with 11 inches also reported in Greenfield and West Springfield Townships. 9.5 inches was reported in Millcreek, with 8 inches in Harborcreek, 7.8 inches in North East, 7.5 inches Northwest Harborcreek, and 6.8 inches in Erie.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A surface trough developed over the Great Lakes on February 20th and lingered into February 21st before surface high pressure worked into the region. The trough focused cold, west-northwest flow across the partially unfrozen waters of Lake Erie, allowing for lake effect snow into Northwest Pennsylvania. This snow began organizing early on the 20th with peak rates during the day and through the evening on the 20th, when snow rates of up to 1 inch per hour occurred. With temperatures in the 10s to low 20s, this snow led to reduced visibility as low as one-quarter of a mile and snow-covered roads. Accidents were reported, with I-90 westbound closed at Exit 16 during the morning of the 20th due to a multi-vehicle accident. This accident resulted in two injuries. Snow gradually lessened in intensity overnight into the morning of the 21st. A peak snowfall of 8 inches was reported by a trained spotter in Harborcreek, with 7.8 inches in Northeast, 7.5 inches in Northwest Harborcreek, and 6.8 inches in Erie. Erie International Airport measured 4.5 inches of snow with this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms rolled in early in the morning of April 4, with storms producing damaging wind gusts across portions of southeastern Pennsylvania. Storms also produced large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong downburst of winds from a severe thunderstorm produced significant damage early Friday morning. The most significant damage occurred in the borough of Media and Nether Providence Township in Delaware County, Pennsylvania. In Media, the most concentrated damage occurred to the northeast of downtown between east State and 4th Streets. Several trees were snapped at their trunks or uprooted. A building at the corner of Jasper and Monroe Streets lost a significant amount of its roof covering. The damage in this area was unidirectional with downed trees and roof coverings pushed to the northeast. In Nether Providence Township, the most significant damage occurred between Providence Road and I-476, south of East Baltimore Pike. Several more trees were uprooted and snapped in this area, falling unidirectional towards the east-southeast. Additional sporadic tree damage was noted between the two concentrated areas of damage. The overall diverging, unidirectional pattern of the damage is consistent with a downburst event. The damage to multiple roofs as well as snapping of trees along their trunks leads to an estimated maximum wind speed of 95-105 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain combined with snow melt and an ice jam to cause minor flooding near Forksville in Sullivan County on March 5, 2005. The ice jam occurred on Mill Creek just above its confluence with Lick Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain combined with snow melt and an ice jam to cause minor flooding near Forksville in Sullivan County on March 5, 2005. The ice jam occurred on Mill Creek just above its confluence with Lick Creek, and was the result of ice becoming jammed in drainage pipes beneath a bridge that allows Mill Creek to flow beneath Millview Park Lane. There was damage to the bridge and pipes, but there was no other property damage reported. This is a common problem area for flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions allowed for severe (D2) drought conditions to continue across southeastern Pennsylvania through March 2025. A prolonged spell of dry conditions across south-central Pennsylvania did allow for severe drought to begin expanding along the Mason-Dixon line towards the end of the month with the most significant precipitation limited to the western half of Pennsylvania during the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Continued dry conditions allowed for D2 (severe) drought conditions to continue across Lebanon County throughout all of March 2025.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front west of central Pennsylvania slowly began to drift eastward during the morning hours and throughout the day on March 16, 2025 allowing ample lift in an environment with sufficient shear and instability to form a quasi-linear convective system across western Pennsylvania. This QLCS continued moving into a more favorable environment across central Pennsylvania, with increased instability from a dry slot coupled with a favorable entrance region of an upper-level jet maximum to produce enhanced shear. This allowed for the QLCS to strengthen across central Pennsylvania and caused extensive damage across west-central Pennsylvania with ample low-level shear spawning two tornadoes in Clinton and Elk counties. Farther east, a less favorable environment was in place with more stable air and lower shear; however, the forward momentum of the QLCS allowed for further damage across northeastern Pennsylvania. Later in the evening, thunderstorms began to form south of central Pennsylvania and gradually drifted northeastward with upper-level support. Strong-to-severe thunderstorms were present along the Mason-Dixon line with a severe thunderstorm producing quarter and ping pong-sized hail in York County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down a tree into wires in Shinglehouse.

Significant structural damage to a barn was also reported. The damage path widened to around 1.6 miles as it neared the end of the track.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front west of central Pennsylvania slowly began to drift eastward during the morning hours and throughout the day on March 16, 2025 allowing ample lift in an environment with sufficient shear and instability to form a quasi-linear convective system across western Pennsylvania. This QLCS continued moving into a more favorable environment across central Pennsylvania, with increased instability from a dry slot coupled with a favorable entrance region of an upper-level jet maximum to produce enhanced shear. Farther east, a less favorable environment was in place with more stable air and lower shear; however, the forward momentum of the QLCS allowed for further damage across northeastern Pennsylvania. Later in the evening, thunderstorms began to form south of central Pennsylvania and gradually drifted northeastward with upper-level support. Strong-to-severe thunderstorms were present along the Mason-Dixon line with a severe thunderstorm producing quarter and ping pong-sized hail in York County.

Farther east, a less favorable environment was in place with more stable air and lower shear; however, the forward momentum of the QLCS allowed for further damage across northeastern Pennsylvania. Later in the evening, thunderstorms began to form south of central Pennsylvania and gradually drifted northeastward with upper-level support. Strong-to-severe thunderstorms were present along the Mason-Dixon line with a severe thunderstorm producing quarter and ping pong-sized hail in York County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms moving through Somerset County producing wind gusts estimated at 70 mph caused widespread damage to trees and powerlines throughout the town of Stoystown. Emergency management reported that there was at least one tree that fell onto a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front west of central Pennsylvania slowly began to drift eastward during the morning hours and throughout the day on March 16, 2025 allowing ample lift in an environment with sufficient shear and instability to form a quasi-linear convective system across western Pennsylvania. This QLCS continued moving into a more favorable environment across central Pennsylvania, with increased instability from a dry slot coupled with a favorable entrance region of an upper-level jet maximum to produce enhanced shear. This allowed for the QLCS to strengthen across central Pennsylvania and caused extensive damage across west-central Pennsylvania with ample low-level shear spawning two tornadoes in Clinton and Elk counties. Farther east, a less favorable environment was in place with more stable air and lower shear; however, the forward momentum of the QLCS allowed for further damage across northeastern Pennsylvania. Later in the evening, thunderstorms began to form south of central Pennsylvania and gradually drifted northeastward with upper-level support. Strong-to-severe thunderstorms were present along the Mason-Dixon line with a severe thunderstorm producing quarter and ping pong-sized hail in York County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS Storm Survey confirmed that a downburst occurred along Haag Hill Road northeast of Olanta in Clearfield County during the afternoon of March 16, 2025. Within this swath, approximately 25-30 trees were either snapped or uprooted in this south-to-north oriented ravine. The trees that fell on the west slope of the ravine were generally oriented towards the north, while trees that fell on the east slope of the ravine were predominantly oriented to the northeast. There were no man-made structures in the path of this downburst, with a residence just east of the ravine appearing to have no damage.

A line of thunderstorms along a prefrontal trough moving across eastern Ohio late in the evening continued into Pennsylvania despite marginal instability. 35 kts of shear packed in lowest 3km was able to maintain the line into western Pennsylvania, and resulted in several reports of damaging winds shortly after midnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damage to a part of a fence, tree branches down, and a destroyed metal shed were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening storm system moved across the area during the afternoon of the 16th along with a potent cold front which led to very strong westerly winds developing that continued into the 17th. A high wind warning was issued and one location recorded a peak wind gust of 58 mph. This storm also brought a mix of snow, sleet, freezing rain and plain rain as it moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds produced a 58 mph gust at KAVP.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure ejected out of the Rocky Mountains and rapidly intensified over the southern Plains on January 5th, tracking east-northeast into the Lower Ohio Valley and into eastern Kentucky on the morning of the 6th. This system brought widespread snow to the region, mostly ending during the evening of the 6th, although a few snow showers lingered into the 7th in the higher elevations.||Regional snow totals were generally greatest south of I-70.  Most snow totals ranged from 1 to 4 inches north of this highway.  Near it and to the south, more reports of 5 to 8 inches were received, with the highest totals in portions of eastern Fayette County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In general, 3.5 to 5.5 inches of snow was reported in the county, with the higher-end totals reported at Eighty Four and Venetia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following the snow event ending January 20th, an anomalously deep trough settled over North America through the 22nd. Temperatures plunged well below normal by the morning of the 21st, with lows in the -5F to -15F range, and wind chill in the -10F to -20F range with isolated colder values.  Cold conditions peaked during the morning of the 22nd as Arctic high pressure settled in, with numerous lows in the -10F to -19F range, with wind chills as low as -25F in some brief cases.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The coldest conditions were noted during the morning of the 22nd. Temperatures dropped as low as -14F, with reported wind chill values as low as -20F on an isolated basis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended period of northwest-flow snow showers occurred between January 7th and January 9th thanks to ongoing cold advection a good fetch of Great Lakes moisture. While snow totals through this event in much of southwest Pennsylvania were spotty and light, reports of 3 to 3.5 inches were received from eastern Fayette County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 3.5 inches of snow was reported near Ohiopyle, with three inches the total near |Champion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strongly forced band of convection developed over West Virginia in the morning ahead of an approaching front, and quickly lifted north-northeastward into Pennsylvania through the early afternoon. Despite relatively modest instability, very strong deep layer shear, along with largely unidirectional southerly flow and elongated hodographs, supported the continuation of significant severe storms. Large impact was felt from straight-line wind, with gusts in the 60 to 80 MPH range, locally higher, causing widespread damage to trees, power lines, and structures. The line of storms continued into Pennsylvania, where the environment was more favorable for tornadic development.  In addition to damaging winds, a number of large hail reports were received, with some stones as large as 1.75 inch diameter. Also, embedded supercells/mesovortices produced five confirmed tornadoes during the afternoon (one of which crossed from Fayette County into Westmoreland County).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked across southern West Virginia and Virginia on the 19th, before riding up the Atlantic Coast into the Canadian Maritimes on the 20th. The Laurel Highlands of Pennsylvania were first hit with frontogenetically-enhanced snow on the 19th, leading to quick accumulation, and then to upslope snow showers in northwest flow behind the system into the 20th. In general, six to ten inches of snow accumulated in the higher elevation. Elsewhere, 1 to 3 inch totals were common, although areas that were affected by lake-effect snow bands saw totals in the 3 to 5 inch range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to six inches of snow fell in the non-ridge areas of Westmoreland County, with the high-end total reported near Mount Pleasant.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough axis crossed into western Ohio late on the 10th and then swung across the Upper Ohio Valley during the morning of the 11th. Moisture associated with a deepening surface low surged northward ahead of the trough.  Snow started across the region during the late afternoon and evening, covering the entire area by midnight and then lingered into Saturday morning. A few northwest flow snow showers lingered into the afternoon and evening, but added little additional snow. Snow totals in southwest Pennsylvania were generally in the 1 to 3 inch range, except portions of Mercer and Venango reached the 3 to 5 inch range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three inches was reported at West Mayfield and Ohioville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strongly forced band of convection developed over West Virginia in the morning ahead of an approaching front, and quickly lifted north-northeastward into Pennsylvania through the early afternoon. Despite relatively modest instability, very strong deep layer shear, along with largely unidirectional southerly flow and elongated hodographs, supported the continuation of significant severe storms. Large impact was felt from straight-line wind, with gusts in the 60 to 80 MPH range, locally higher, causing widespread damage to trees, power lines, and structures. The line of storms continued into Pennsylvania, where the environment was more favorable for tornadic development.  In addition to damaging winds, a number of large hail reports were received, with some stones as large as 1.75 inch diameter. Also, embedded supercells/mesovortices produced five confirmed tornadoes during the afternoon (one of which crossed from Fayette County into Westmoreland County).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strongly forced band of convection developed over West Virginia in the morning ahead of an approaching front, and quickly lifted north-northeastward into Pennsylvania through the early afternoon. Despite relatively modest instability, very strong deep layer shear, along with largely unidirectional southerly flow and elongated hodographs, supported the continuation of significant severe storms. Large impact was felt from straight-line wind, with gusts in the 60 to 80 MPH range, locally higher, causing widespread damage to trees, power lines, and structures. The line of storms continued into Pennsylvania, where the environment was more favorable for tornadic development.  In addition to damaging winds, a number of large hail reports were received, with some stones as large as 1.75 inch diameter. Also, embedded supercells/mesovortices produced five confirmed tornadoes during the afternoon (one of which crossed from Fayette County into Westmoreland County).

An area of departing surface low-pressure coupled with an enhanced upper-level trough dipping down across the eastern United States allowed for an extended period of cold air across central Pennsylvania beginning January 20, 2025. Gradient winds aloft allowed for breezy winds beginning on January 20 and continuing through January 22, 2025, which allowed for dangerously cold wind chills to settle in across much of central Pennsylvania. A transient area of surface high pressure on the evening of January 21, 2025 allowed for less cloud cover, leading to more widespread cold conditions, allowing for all of central Pennsylvania to experience well-below average temperatures with all locations dropping below zero wind chills through the morning of January 22. This extended period of cold temperatures caused two fatalities: one in Elk Lick Township (Somerset County) and another in Johnstown (Cambria County), both deaths were attributed to hypothermia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An extended period of well-below average temperatures across Clearfield County allowed for wind chills to generally range between -15 to -20 degrees. The lowest wind chill was observed at Clearfield-Lawrence Airport, where a wind chill of -19 degrees was observed during the early morning hours of January 21, 2025.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure off the Mid-Atlantic Coast began to form during the morning hours of January 19, 2025 allowing for ample lift and moisture to begin across eastern Pennsylvania generally tapering off during the late evening hours. Low to mid level frontogenesis allowed for a band of heavier snow to form across portions of Lancaster, York, and Lebanon counties that began to shift northeastward into Schuylkill County during the early afternoon hours. The highest snowfall totals across eastern Pennsylvania were observed across Lebanon County, where snowfall totals generally ranged between 8 to 9 inches with slightly lower totals observed across Lancaster and York counties. Enhanced westerly flow with the coastal low stationed east of central Pennsylvania allowed for orographically induced snowfall to continue across portions of Somerset County into the morning hours of January 20, 2025 bringing localized higher totals in upslope locations, where observations exceeded 11 inches, with 15.7 inches of snowfall was observed in Laurel Summit.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced 5 to 7 inches of snow across Adams County, with snowfall beginning during the morning hours of January 19, 2025 and continuing through the evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent shortwave along the base of an upper-level longwave trough began dropping southward across Pennsylvania during the late evening hours of January 27, 2025 and into the early morning hours of January 28, 2025. Locally heavy snow showers began to develop across western Pennsylvania during the morning hours of January 28 before encountering marginal instability in central Pennsylvania, providing a favorable environment for snow squalls. These snow squalls began approaching Interstate 80, where snow squalls were observed before moving into an environment with less instability, thus allowing for snow squalls to weaken as they progressed into east-central Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. A snow squall impacted State Route 6 in Potter County on the morning of January 28, 2025. An RWIS station on US-6 near Coudersport recorded a visibility drop to below 1/4 mile as the squall moved through and the camera at the station showed snow quickly accumulating on the roadway.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An extended period of well-below average temperatures across Cambria County allowed for wind chills to generally range between -15 to -20 degrees during the early morning hours of January 21, 2025 with temperatures remaining well-below average throughout the daytime hours. The lowest wind chill across the county was reported at Johnstown Airport, where a wind chill of -25 degrees was observed during the early morning hours of January 22, 2025. In Johnstown, one fatality was reported when a 59-year-old man froze in his residence, found after a welfare check. The temperature inside the home was reported to be 18 degrees when the check was made by law enforcement.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of departing surface low-pressure coupled with an enhanced upper-level trough dipping down across the eastern United States allowed for an extended period of cold air across central Pennsylvania beginning January 20, 2025. Gradient winds aloft allowed for breezy winds beginning on January 20 and continuing through January 22, 2025, which allowed for dangerously cold wind chills to settle in across much of central Pennsylvania. A transient area of surface high pressure on the evening of January 21, 2025 allowed for less cloud cover, leading to more widespread cold conditions, allowing for all of central Pennsylvania to experience well-below average temperatures with all locations dropping below zero wind chills through the morning of January 22. This extended period of cold temperatures caused two fatalities: one in Elk Lick Township (Somerset County) and another in Johnstown (Cambria County), both deaths were attributed to hypothermia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An extended period of well-below average temperatures across Fulton County allowed for wind chills to generally range between -10 to -15 degrees. The lowest wind chill of -20 degrees was reported near Crystal Springs during the early morning hours of January 22, 2025.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent shortwave along the base of an upper-level longwave trough began dropping southward across Pennsylvania during the late evening hours of January 27, 2025 and into the early morning hours of January 28, 2025. Locally heavy snow showers began to develop across western Pennsylvania during the morning hours of January 28 before encountering marginal instability in central Pennsylvania, providing a favorable environment for snow squalls. These snow squalls began approaching Interstate 80, where snow squalls were observed before moving into an environment with less instability, thus allowing for snow squalls to weaken as they progressed into east-central Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. A snow squall moved through Sullivan County on the morning of January 28, 2025. An RWIS station on US-220 southbound near Loyalsock Creek recorded a visibility drop to near 1/4 mile and the camera at the station showed snow quickly accumulating on the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning hours of January 29, 2025, a potent Arctic Front began dropping southeast into Pennsylvania and became collocated with the left exit region of a strong upper-level jet. These factors, coupled with marginal surface-based instability, allowed for heavier snow showers and snow squalls to form by mid-morning. Dynamics and sufficient lift were most favorable in the northern half of the Commonwealth, thus snow squall potential dropped fairly rapidly south of the Interstate 80 corridor. Multiple snow squalls were observed; however, no injuries and/or fatalities were observed due to traffic accidents in snow squalls. Gusty winds ahead of the cold frontal passage knocked over multiple trees into wires in Perry County, closing West Main Street for a period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds with estimated wind gusts near 40 mph knocked down a trees into wires, knocked both down into the roadway near West Main Street in Harrison Township, closing the roadway in both directions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain resulted in some ice accretion within the Lehigh Valley and southern Pocono region of Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light glaze of ice occurred across lower elevations of the county below 1,200 feet and in some higher elevation valleys. The highest reported measurement was 0.02 from Mount Pocono.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strongly forced band of convection developed over West Virginia in the morning ahead of an approaching front, and quickly lifted north-northeastward into Pennsylvania through the early afternoon. Despite relatively modest instability, very strong deep layer shear, along with largely unidirectional southerly flow and elongated hodographs, supported the continuation of significant severe storms. Large impact was felt from straight-line wind, with gusts in the 60 to 80 MPH range, locally higher, causing widespread damage to trees, power lines, and structures. The line of storms continued into Pennsylvania, where the environment was more favorable for tornadic development.  In addition to damaging winds, a number of large hail reports were received, with some stones as large as 1.75 inch diameter. Also, embedded supercells/mesovortices produced five confirmed tornadoes during the afternoon (one of which crossed from Fayette County into Westmoreland County).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure formed over the western Carolinas along a cold front on Sunday, the 19th. As this low moved toward the mid-Atlantic coast, the northern and western edge of the snow made it into Northeast PA during the late morning hours. Snow became steadier during the afternoon as the low moved offshore and began to strengthen. The snow tapered off and came to an end during the evening hours as the as the low continued to intensify and track to the northeast off the coast of New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated between 3 and 7 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface cold front with marginal instability allowed for snow squalls and bands of heavy snow showers to form across central Pennsylvania during the evening hours of January 3, 2025. These snow squalls were exacerbated by road temperatures just above freezing ahead of the squalls, allowing for road temperatures to quickly drop below freezing and flash freeze potential to increase as the event progressed. A pile-up occurred on Interstate 81 in Lebanon County, with one resultant injury reported. Heavy snow bands also caused incidents in Cumberland and Centre Counties, with no injuries reported from these incidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. A snow squall impacted portions of Clearfield County on the afternoon of January 3rd, 2025. The RWIS station near Penfield on Interstate 80 reported a visibility drop to less than 1/4 mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Enhanced northwesterly flow in the wake of a surface cold front stationed east of Pennsylvania, coupled with marginal surface-based CAPE allowed for locally heavier snow bands and squalls to form across western Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening hours of January 1, 2025. A snow squall was observed in Cambria County, with webcams and observations outlining a sharp decline in visibility and roads quickly becoming snow-covered with temperatures well-below freezing at precipitation onset.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. An RWIS sensor on US Route 22 near Cresson reported a sharp drop in visibility below a quarter-mile, with a slight drop in surface and road temperatures. During the snow squall, roads became snow-covered quickly with sub-freezing temperatures at onset.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An extended period of well-below average temperatures across Snyder County allowed for wind chills to generally range between -15 to -20 degrees across much of the county, including at Penn Valley Airport in Selinsgrove.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An extended period of well-below average temperatures across Somerset County allowed for wind chills to generally range between -20 to -25 degrees during the early morning hours of January 21, 2025 with temperatures remaining well-below average throughout the daytime hours. Colder air began to settle in during the evening hours of January 21st with temperatures dropping steadily overnight, allowing for wind chills to range between -20 and -25 degrees across much of the county. The lowest wind chill across the county was reported in Meyersdale, where a wind chill of -27 degrees was observed during the early morning hours of January 22, 2025. This period of extreme cold resulted in one fatality, where a 73-year-old man in Elk Lick Township was found dead in his home with no heating on January 22, 2025 after not being able to be contacted for multiple days.

A transient area of surface high pressure on the evening of January 21, 2025 allowed for less cloud cover, leading to more widespread cold conditions, allowing for all of central Pennsylvania to experience well-below average temperatures with all locations dropping below zero wind chills through the morning of January 22. This extended period of cold temperatures caused two fatalities: one in Elk Lick Township (Somerset County) and another in Johnstown (Cambria County), both deaths were attributed to hypothermia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An extended period of well-below average temperatures across York County allowed for wind chills to generally range between -15 to -20 degrees across much of the county. The lowest reported wind chill was observed at York Airport, where a wind chill of -23 degrees was observed during the early morning hours of January 22, 2025.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface cold front with marginal instability allowed for snow squalls and bands of heavy snow showers to form across central Pennsylvania during the evening hours of January 3, 2025. These snow squalls were exacerbated by road temperatures just above freezing ahead of the squalls, allowing for road temperatures to quickly drop below freezing and flash freeze potential to increase as the event progressed. A pile-up occurred on Interstate 81 in Lebanon County, with one resultant injury reported. Heavy snow bands also caused incidents in Cumberland and Centre Counties, with no injuries reported from these incidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. A snow squall impacted portions of Interstate 80 in Southern Clinton County during the evening of January 3rd, 2025. The RWIS station at mile marker 190 of Interstate 80 reported a visibility drop to below 1/4 of a mile as the squall moved through.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure stationed south of Pennsylvania gradually progressed east during the day on January 6, 2025 with enhanced upper flow allowing for a significant surge of moisture into the area. Snowfall began during the early morning hours across the Laurel Highlands with precipitation struggling to make it further east until after sunrise, with the bulk of snowfall being limited to the southern tier where ample moisture was collocated with the best frontogenetic forcing. Enhanced westerly flow behind the shortwave was optimal for orographically induced snowfall to continue across Somerset and Cambria Counties into the late evening hours. The heaviest snowfall from this event was located across Somerset County, where upslope locations received up to 11 inches of snowfall with much of the county receiving between 5 to 7 inches of snowfall. Further east, observations ranged between 2 to 4 inches of snowfall across much of the southern tier of Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced 5 to 7 inches of snow across Somerset County with localized amounts of 11 inches across the higher elevations of Somerset County from the early morning hours and throughout the day of January 7, 2025.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought heavy banded snow to portions of Delmarva and southern New Jersey, with lighter snowfall amounts tapering off to the north into the Philadelphia metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snowfall with totals ranging from 1-2 occurred across lower Buck County. The highest report was 2.0 from near Langhorne.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions allowed for severe (D2) drought conditions to continue across southeastern Pennsylvania through January 2025. Multiple snow storms traversing central Pennsylvania brought snowfall accumulations to the area towards the middle to end of the month; however, the long duration of dry conditions leading up to these systems allowed for drought to persist through the entire month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Continued dry conditions allowed for D2 (severe) drought conditions to continue across Lancaster County throughout all of January 2025.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure off the Mid-Atlantic Coast began to form during the morning hours of January 19, 2025 allowing for ample lift and moisture to begin across eastern Pennsylvania generally tapering off during the late evening hours. Low to mid level frontogenesis allowed for a band of heavier snow to form across portions of Lancaster, York, and Lebanon counties that began to shift northeastward into Schuylkill County during the early afternoon hours. The highest snowfall totals across eastern Pennsylvania were observed across Lebanon County, where snowfall totals generally ranged between 8 to 9 inches with slightly lower totals observed across Lancaster and York counties. Enhanced westerly flow with the coastal low stationed east of central Pennsylvania allowed for orographically induced snowfall to continue across portions of Somerset County into the morning hours of January 20, 2025 bringing localized higher totals in upslope locations, where observations exceeded 11 inches, with 15.7 inches of snowfall was observed in Laurel Summit.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced 5 to 7 inches of snow across Franklin County, with snowfall beginning during the early morning hours of January 19, 2025 and continuing through the evening.

Enhanced westerly flow with the coastal low stationed east of central Pennsylvania allowed for orographically induced snowfall to continue across portions of Somerset County into the morning hours of January 20, 2025 bringing localized higher totals in upslope locations, where observations exceeded 11 inches, with 15.7 inches of snowfall was observed in Laurel Summit.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced 5 to 6 inches of snow across Cumberland County, with higher elevations of the county receiving up to 7 inches of snowfall accumulation. Snowfall with this winter storm began during the morning hours of January 19, 2025 and continuing through the evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure west of Pennsylvania during the evening hours of February 15, 2025 slowly drifted eastward into central Pennsylvania throughout the day on February 16th. The area of low pressure began to stall out across central Pennsylvania and deepen, allowing for an increased pressure gradient to set up across the area, with ample moisture being advected from the Atlantic Ocean under easterly winds. This allowed for widespread snow showers to begin across central Pennsylvania on February 16th during the early morning hours and continuing into the evening hours. Strong to high winds were observed across central Pennsylvania, highest across the Laurel Highlands with descending westerly flow from higher elevations and the Lower Susquehanna Valley near the tightening pressure gradient as the low pressure system continued to strengthen as it began moving offshore the New Jersey coast. In Somerset County, a combination of upslope moist flow and the tightening pressure gradient caused multiple hours of low visibilities from snowfall and blowing snow along with multiple hours with winds gusting over 40 mph, meeting blizzard conditions during the evening hours of February 16th. Damage from winds was observed across the Lower Susquehanna Valley, along with Bedford and Blair Counties where downsloping winds knocked down multiple trees and wires, knocking out power for portions of these counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of high winds throughout the evening areas were observed near Harper Tavern, with winds peaking at 58 mph between 6pm and 8pm. A period of strong winds also occurred throughout the early afternoon hours of February 16, 2025 and into the early morning hours of February 17th. High winds knocked down multiple poles, trees, and wires down along State Route 22.

High winds knocked down a large tree onto Meadow Lane near Hanover Heights, closing the roadway for multiple hours. A large tree fell down near the intersection of Swatara Road and Laudermilch Road in Hershey, closing the roadway. A tree also fell down, with multiple branches covering train tracks south of Middletown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strongly forced band of convection developed over West Virginia in the morning ahead of an approaching front, and quickly lifted north-northeastward into Pennsylvania through the early afternoon. Despite relatively modest instability, very strong deep layer shear, along with largely unidirectional southerly flow and elongated hodographs, supported the continuation of significant severe storms. Large impact was felt from straight-line wind, with gusts in the 60 to 80 MPH range, locally higher, causing widespread damage to trees, power lines, and structures. The line of storms continued into Pennsylvania, where the environment was more favorable for tornadic development.  In addition to damaging winds, a number of large hail reports were received, with some stones as large as 1.75 inch diameter. Also, embedded supercells/mesovortices produced five confirmed tornadoes during the afternoon (one of which crossed from Fayette County into Westmoreland County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A porch roof was lifted off of a house in Carroll Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Occluding surface low pressure tracked across the Upper Ohio Valley and into western New York by the morning of January 1st. Rain transitioned to snow behind a crossing cold front in strong cold advection. Lake enhancement led to accumulations of 1 to 4 inches across some locations north of I-80.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An accumulation of 2.5 inches occurred at Tionesta, with two inches or less elsewhere in the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of high winds throughout the afternoon and evening hours occurred across Bedford County, with winds gusts estimated to peak near 60 mph between 4pm and 7pm on February 16, 2025. These high winds produced widespread tree damage, with multiple roadways closed across the county into the morning hours of February 17. Trees and powerlines closed Woodbury Pike and Raystown Road in Hopewell, French Creek Road and Battleground Hollow Road in Everett, Lincoln Highway east of Bedford, Chaneysville Road southeast of Rainsburg, Cross Cove Road east of Claysburg, and at the intersection of Winding Ridge Road and Menchtown Road southwest of Breezewood. Strong winds continued into the early morning hours of February 17, knocking down trees near New Paris and Lake Gordon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure began to approach western Pennsylvania during the late evening hours of February 8th and into the morning hours of February 9, 2025 with an occluded front beginning to set up across southwestern Pennsylvania. A narrow, yet robust, warm nose aloft allowed for freezing rain to begin across the Laurel Highlands as precipitation entered the area, with a transition to rain occurring as temperatures began to warm by the late morning hours. This low pressure system stalled briefly before rapidly moving offshore by the late afternoon hours of February 9th. Ice accumulations were significant across Somerset and Cambria counties, with Cambria County reporting approximately 1,500 power outages from ice on powerlines and fallen limbs onto powerlines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice storm impacted Somerset County on February 8, 2025. Ice accumulation of up to 0.25 inches occurred over much of the county with higher amounts reported. The ice resulted in over 1500 power outages. Light snow also occurred with this storm, though snow accumulation was generally less than 0.5 inches across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions allowed for severe (D2) drought conditions to continue across southeastern Pennsylvania through February 2025. Multiple rounds of precipitation did allow for some relief; however, long-term precipitation deficits kept much of southeastern Pennsylvania in severe drought. Dry conditions towards the end of the month did allow for slight expansion of severe drought in portions of York County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Continued dry conditions allowed for D2 (severe) drought conditions to continue across Lancaster County throughout all of February 2025.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure transitioned from southwest Pennsylvania into upstate New York over the course of the day. . EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front stationed west of central Pennsylvania began slowly lifting northward on February 2, 2025 providing ample lift with easterly winds allowing for Atlantic moisture to advect into Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening hours. Snow occurred at the onset across the southern half of Pennsylvania before temperatures began to warm enough for precipitation to transition into rain, allowing for a light glaze of ice to form on elevated and untreated surfaces. In Lancaster County, icy roads caused a vehicle to slide off of the roadway, sending four people to local hospitals for medical evaluation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of mixed precipitation, including snow and freezing rain, produced icy roadways in portions of Lancaster County during the evening of February 2, 2025. A vehicle went off the roadway on the northbound lane of US Route 222 with four patients transported to local hospital with one person being ejected from the vehicle. This closed the roadway into the early morning hours of February 3, 2025.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low-level jet entrance region across western Pennsylvania, with moist westerly flow along the Alleghenies providing sufficient lift, allowed for snow showers to occur across northern and western portions of Pennsylvania during the early morning hours of February 14, 2025. Localized snow squalls occurred in an environment with marginally sufficient surface-based instability, mainly across the northern tier of Pennsylvania. A snow squall occurred during the early morning hours on Interstate 80 in Montour County, causing multiple accidents that resulted in two fatalities and two injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. A snow squall allowed for a period of heavy snow, lowered visibilities, and breezy winds to occur on Interstate 80 in Montour County during the early morning hours of February 14, 2025. This snow squall produced slick roadways that resulted in an accident involving multiple commercial and passenger (estimated between seven to eight) vehicles. There were two fatalities and two injuries from this crash. A second traffic accident occurred in the backlog of traffic, with a vehicle under a tractor trailer, resulting in another fatality. The westbound lane of Interstate 80 was closed from the time of the incident through much of the day, with traffic being rerouted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter system brought widespread freezing rain, sleet, and some snow to much of the region. Precipitation began the evening of the 8th and continued through early in the morning of the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wintry mix of snow, sleet, and freezing rain occurred. Trace amounts of snow and sleet were reported nearby. The greatest freezing rain measurement was 0.15 at the Pottstown Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure west of Pennsylvania during the evening hours of February 15, 2025 slowly drifted eastward into central Pennsylvania throughout the day on February 16th. The area of low pressure began to stall out across central Pennsylvania and deepen, allowing for an increased pressure gradient to set up across the area, with ample moisture being advected from the Atlantic Ocean under easterly winds. This allowed for widespread snow showers to begin across central Pennsylvania on February 16th during the early morning hours and continuing into the evening hours. Strong to high winds were observed across central Pennsylvania, highest across the Laurel Highlands with descending westerly flow from higher elevations and the Lower Susquehanna Valley near the tightening pressure gradient as the low pressure system continued to strengthen as it began moving offshore the New Jersey coast. In Somerset County, a combination of upslope moist flow and the tightening pressure gradient caused multiple hours of low visibilities from snowfall and blowing snow along with multiple hours with winds gusting over 40 mph, meeting blizzard conditions during the evening hours of February 16th. Damage from winds was observed across the Lower Susquehanna Valley, along with Bedford and Blair Counties where downsloping winds knocked down multiple trees and wires, knocking out power for portions of these counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winter weather caused slick roads in southern Clinton County, causing a tractor trailer to jackknife and flip on its side near mile marker 190 on the eastbound lane of Interstate 80. The driver sustained injuries from the incident and the roadway was closed for approximately two hours during the early morning hours of February 16, 2025.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low-level jet approaching west-central Pennsylvania, coupled with a shortwave trough, allowed for ample warm air advection in the lower-levels with relatively cold air at the surface. This allowed for freezing rain and freezing drizzle to occur across the Laurel Highlands beginning during the evening hours of February 5, 2025 and continuing into the morning of February 6th. Significant icing began to occur across Cambria County during this timeframe with portions of Lancaster County also experiencing ice accumulation. A quick warming trend in temperatures emerged during the late morning hours of February 6th, allowing for a quick transition back into rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice storm impacted Cambria County on February 6, 2025. Total freezing rain accumulation generally ranged from 0.15 inches to 0.3 inches, though locally higher amounts were reported. The largest ice accumulation was reported at the John Murtha Johnstown-Cambria County Airport where the ASOS measured 0.38 inches of ice.

Trees and wires were also reported on Cooks Hollow Road north of Tyrone, at the intersections of 17th Street and Valley View Road in Altoona, at the intersection of Rider Farm Road and Gwin Road in Altoona, and along Old Route 22 west of Duncansville. There were also multiple large trees reported down across Juniata River Road near Canoe Creek State Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Occluding surface low pressure tracked across the Upper Ohio Valley and into western New York by the morning of January 1st. Rain transitioned to snow behind a crossing cold front in strong cold advection. Lake enhancement led to accumulations of 1 to 4 inches across some locations north of I-80.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Accumulations of 4.7 inches were reported near Titusville and Cooperstown, with 1 to 2 inches reported closer to Franklin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought persisted into early May across portions of southeastern Pennsylvania. However, an anomalously wet May 2025 (the wettest May on record at Harrisburg, PA) allowed conditions to improve dramatically as the month progressed. By the end of May, all of the D2 conditions had been removed from Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought continued for eastern Adams County until the U.S. Drought Monitor update on May 20, 2025, when all remaining D2 conditions were removed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strongly forced band of convection developed over West Virginia in the morning ahead of an approaching front, and quickly lifted north-northeastward into Pennsylvania through the early afternoon. Despite relatively modest instability, very strong deep layer shear, along with largely unidirectional southerly flow and elongated hodographs, supported the continuation of significant severe storms. Large impact was felt from straight-line wind, with gusts in the 60 to 80 MPH range, locally higher, causing widespread damage to trees, power lines, and structures. The line of storms continued into Pennsylvania, where the environment was more favorable for tornadic development.  In addition to damaging winds, a number of large hail reports were received, with some stones as large as 1.75 inch diameter. Also, embedded supercells/mesovortices produced five confirmed tornadoes during the afternoon (one of which crossed from Fayette County into Westmoreland County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A roof was blown off of a farmer's market.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two separate showers very close to a stationary front each produced a landspout tornado on the afternoon of May 4.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video via social media confirms a brief landspout and/or waterspout occurred near the northern end of Blue Marsh Lake in the Blue Marsh National Recreation area near the community of Mount Pleasant. An initial storm survey did not find any evidence of damage in this area. It is plausible that the tornadic circulation occurred entirely over water, or in an inaccessible area of land. Path length and width is estimated based on the available video.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two separate showers very close to a stationary front each produced a landspout tornado on the afternoon of May 4.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple pictures and videos showed a tornado in a rural area between Mohrsville and Shoemakersville. The storm survey concluded that the tornadic circulation began in an open field near Main Street and Shoey Road, where a line of swirl marks from ground scouring was noted for about a half mile. The circulation weakened some and became discontinuous as it progressed east toward Pottsville Pike (Route 61), crossing the Schuylkill River. A photo from social media showed the tornadic circulation crossing the highway near a railroad crossing south of Shoemakersville Road, where some minor tree damage occurred. The circulation then  continued northeast, crossing Shoemakersville Road causing minor tree damage where it ended.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An occluding low pressure system was over the region resulting in scattered severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS storm survey determined an EF1 tornado with estimated maximum wind speeds of 80 to 90 mph occurred near Bangor. The first damage was noted at a Public Works Barn. Damage in this area was consistent with maximum wind speeds of 70 to 80 mph. A small tree was felled and several tiles were blown off the roof. Of note, some of the tiles from the roof were found approximately one quarter mile away near a school. For the next approximate mile, discontinuous tree damage occurred in a wooded area. The highest degree of damage was noted behind a residence on |Washington Boulevard. At that location, several softwood trees were uprooted and a few were snapped. Maximum wind speed in this area were estimated to be 80 to 90 mph. This is also the location where the estimated maximum width was observed, approximately 100 yards. Further tree damage was noted where the tornado crossed the train tracks near the intersection of Mill Street and Murray Street. The final damage was noted with some tiles blown off a historic commercial building at the intersection of Murray Street and W Messinger Street.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado developed within a squall line, near Haddenville, PA in Fayette County on March 16th. Initial EF0 damage, consisting of uprooted hardwood trees was noted along Stoney Point Road, north of the intersection with New Salem Road. The tornado then tracked to the northeast between Stoney Point and Kaider Roads possibly touching down and lifting several times as damage was more sporadic through the first half mile of its track. The tornado intensified as it neared Sunset Way, where EF1 damage was noted. This included a front door and windows being blown in on a newly completed house, loss of shingles and siding, trees uprooted and the flattening of a small shed. The tornado advanced down a hill and crossed Kaider Road near Kurutz Lane. A single family residence near this intersection had several windows broken and a portion of its |roof was torn off and blown into a tree line some 500 feet away. The same tree line saw multiple trees snapped and uprooted. The tornado then weakened as it approached Haddenville Road, where EF0 damage was noted including: loss of shingles, a softwood tree snapped and multiple trees damaged and uprooted. The tornado began to dissipate |near Highway 40.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storage garage along Industrial Park Road appears to have had a portion of its roof structure uplifted and removed, based on a photo provided. Trees also were reported down in the area. This location is coincident with an intense mesovortex. Based on drone footage of the garage and downstream forests, the cause is likely straight-line wind as damage is limited to the garage, with only a couple of snapped trees, and all of this damage was blown in the direction of the prevailing wind.

The tornado dissipated near Two Mile Run Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down in a field near Barrondale Rd., causing partial collapse of a barn and uplifting the roof of an outbuilding before moving into a forested area and causing sporadic hardwood tree damage. Damage became more consistent along a ridge line immediately south of Luciusboro Rd. The tornado crossed the road, snapping the tops of softwood trees before uplifting the roof and collapsing the walls of a second|barn. Debris from the second barn was lofted and ejected to the left of the track. The tornado continued into the woods and to the next ridge line, uprooting a cluster of large hardwoods and snapping multiple trunks. A portion of the barn roof was deposited near the ridge line, over 300 yards to the north. Peak estimated wind speed of the tornado was 105 MPH.

The line of storms continued into Pennsylvania, where the environment was more favorable for tornadic development.  In addition to damaging winds, a number of large hail reports were received, with some stones as large as 1.75 inch diameter. Also, embedded supercells/mesovortices produced five confirmed tornadoes during the afternoon (one of which crossed from Fayette County into Westmoreland County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured at Arnold Palmer Regional Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area low pressure moved over the Great Lakes region and a warm front lifting north early in the day put the area into the warm sector of the system, ahead of an approaching cold front. While instability was limited, there was strong wind shear in place, and a line of gusty showers and thunderstorms along and ahead of the cold front produced locally damaging wind gusts. There was also a tight pressure gradient in place which resulted in strong, locally damaging non-thunderstorm synoptic winds prior to the cold frontal passage as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree on Cook Road in Franklindale.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado developed within a squall line, impacting the Chestnut Ridge region of Fayette and Westmoreland Counties. Initial EF0 damage, characterized by snapped hardwood trees, was observed near Breakneck and Wingrove Roads. The tornado then tracked northeast, crossing Bear Rocks and County Line Road, where it crossed into Westmoreland County. Estimated peak wind for the Fayette County portion of the tornado was 80 MPH.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This EF1 tornado developed within a squall line, impacting the Chestnut Ridge region of Fayette and Westmoreland Counties. The tornado, causing EF0 damage in Fayette County, caused more significant damage after crossing County Line Road and then Acme Dams Road. This included snapped large pine trees, power poles tilted at a 45-degree angle, the loss of a carport roof from a residence, and a flipped RV trailer. As the tornado crossed Claypike Road, a power pole snapped, and an outdoor structure sustained 20% roof damage. The tornado began to weaken as it ascended a hillside near Stairs Road, dissipating near Donegal Lake. However, it subsequently re-intensified a few miles downstream, between Mansville and Ligonier PA as a new tornado touchdown. Maximum estimated wind speed for this portion of the tornado track was 100 MPH.

A multi-day rain event caused by a slow-moving upper-level low resulted in 1-2.5 inches of rain across central Pennsylvania. Clearfield County saw the most rain with this system, with Coal Run near DuBois briefly leaving it's banks and temporarily closing one road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flowing Well Road near DuBois was temporarily closed between Route 255 and Sher De Lin Road due to overflow from Coal Run during a multi-day rain event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A narrow stripe of heavy rain lifted northward across central Adams County the morning of May 15, 2025, producing localized flash flooding issues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Localized heavy rain led to a bridge being washed out near the intersection of York Road and Centennial Road in Straban Township.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain led to the intersection of Swift Run and New Chester roads in Straban Township becoming flooded and impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep stream of moisture was pulled northwestward from the Atlantic Ocean into the mountains of south-central Pennsylvania around an upper level low centered over the Ohio Valley during the afternoon and evening hours of May 13. 2025. This led to extensive flooding and flash flooding across Somerset County and southwestern Bedford County, and more sporadic flooding from the remainder of Bedford County eastward into Fulton and Franklin counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A several hour period of moderate to heavy rainfall led to multiple road closures in the Meyersdale area, as well the stranding of a train at the railroad crossing at Peterbrink Road south of Garrett.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A several hour period of moderate to heavy rainfall led to a landslide near Boynton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure stationed across the Delmarva during the early morning hours of May 6, 2025 began to lift north and east during the afternoon hours, allowing portions of eastern Pennsylvania to have a brief period of residence time in the warm sector. This residence time allowed for destabilization ahead of a surface cold front, with ample shear in place to allow for a line of showers and thunderstorms to slowly lift north and east through the region during the afternoon and evening hours. Severe thunderstorms associated with the line caused numerous tree damage in the Lower Susquehanna Valley, with a microburst producing a concentrated area of damage west of Orwigsburg consisting of snapped and uprooted trees. Heavy rain continued further north, where already saturated soils allowed for isolated instances of flooding closing roadways in Lycoming and Tioga counties. Heavy rainfall in Dauphin County also caused flash flooding, closing multiple roadways in Harrisburg during rush hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced a measured wind gust of 64 mph in Myerstown. Several tree limbs were also knocked down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deepening surface low pressure stationed across southern Pennsylvania during the late evening hours of May 30 into the early morning hours of May 31, 2025 allowed for a period of heavy rainfall and gusty winds. Initial heavy rainfall across the southern tier led to saturation of soils, before a tightening pressure gradient behind the area of low pressure brought a three-to-four-hour period of strong winds. These strong winds knocked down multiple trees in Lancaster, Dauphin, Centre, and Perry counties. In Cumberland County, widespread tree damage was observed with strong winds and a tree falling into a residence in Wormleysburg. Multiple trees and wires were downed bringing power outages to Carlisle. with wind gusts as high as 50 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong wind gusts estimated at 40 to 50 mph knocked trees and powerlines down across Cumberland County. Wertzville Road was closed between Sample Bridge and Beard roads after trees and wires fell onto the roadway. One tree landed on a residence in Wormleysburg Borough. Additionally, numerous trees were knocked down on the east side of Carlisle. Widespread power outages occurred, with approximately 9000 customers losing power.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog with visibility 1/4 mile or less was observed for several hours at Trenton-Mercer Airport, Northeast Philadelphia Airport, Pennridge Airport and Quakertown Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ahead of a surface trough, 1000 to 2000 J/kg of surface-based CAPE was able to develop during the afternoon of May 2nd, combined with 30 knots of 0-6km shear. This combination supported the development of mainly discrete storms over eastern Ohio into southwest PA, before storms began to congeal into clusters.  A few hail reports of up to 1.25 inch diameter were received, along with a number of wind damage reports.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front across northwestern Pennsylvania allowed for severe thunderstorms to form during the early morning hours across northwestern Pennsylvania, briefly producing quarter-sized hail before moving northeastward into New York. Thunderstorms associated with a cold front across Ohio and western Pennsylvania continued progressing further eastward, becoming more separated from the surface cold front as they progressed into central Pennsylvania during the afternoon hours of May 16, 2025. Enhanced destabilization across central Pennsylvania with ample shear in place allowed for thunderstorms to continue to maintain themselves as they continued into the afternoon/evening hours, with isolated supercells causing damage across the south-central mountains and across the southern tier during the evening hours. Steep lapse rates also allowed for supercells to produce large hail, with half-dollar sized hail in McAlevys Fort. Locally heavy rain from thunderstorms, coupled with saturated soils allowed for isolated instances of flooding to occur across Potter and Schuylkill counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain of only 0.75 in an hour fell onto wet ground and led to reports of water across some roadways in the vicinity of Galeton and Germania.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Prolonged dry conditions with low relative humidity values and gusty winds allowed for multiple wildfires to begin during the afternoon of April 23, 2025 in Michaux State Forest, located in southwestern Cumberland County. The wildfire originated along Shippensburg Road between Milesburn and Thompson Road and expanded quickly in size to around 2,248 acres at the wildfire's furthest extent. At the fire's peak, aerial support was enlisted to battle the fire, along with firefighting crews on the ground. Evacuations from structures began during the afternoon hours of April 24th and continued through April 25th with 20-30 residences evacuated. While the bulk of wildfire damage was contained in April 2025, wildfires continued into early May 2025. The Thompson Hollow and Hammond Rocks wildfires caused approximately $1,100,000 in tree damage along with $800,000 in suppression costs from the beginning of the wildfires in April 2025 through the early stages of May 2025.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire developed during the afternoon of April 23, 2025 in Michaux State Forest, located in southwestern Cumberland County. The wildfire originated along Shippensburg Road between Milesburn and Thompson Road. The wildfire occurred during a stretch of unseasonably dry weather along with gusty winds, and expanded quickly in size to around 2,248 acres at the wildfire's furthest extent. At the fire's peak, aerial support was enlisted to battle the fire, along with firefighting crews on the ground. Evacuations from structures began during the afternoon hours of April 24th and continued through April 25th with 20-30 residences evacuated. This wildfire also caused hazy/smokey conditions across Interstate 81 between mile markers 29 and 59 in both directions. Containment of the Thompson Hollow Wildfire began to increase late in the month but the fire continued to burn into the beginning of May 2025. ||At the same time as the Thompson Hollow Wildfire, the Hammond Rocks Wildfire also ignited during the afternoon hours of April 24th, also burning 470 acres within the Michaux State Forest. Kings Gap State Park was evacuated due to the Hammond Rocks Wildfire, with aerial support also needed to contain the second wildfire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front across northwestern Pennsylvania allowed for severe thunderstorms to form during the early morning hours across northwestern Pennsylvania, briefly producing quarter-sized hail before moving northeastward into New York. Thunderstorms associated with a cold front across Ohio and western Pennsylvania continued progressing further eastward, becoming more separated from the surface cold front as they progressed into central Pennsylvania during the afternoon hours of May 16, 2025. Enhanced destabilization across central Pennsylvania with ample shear in place allowed for thunderstorms to continue to maintain themselves as they continued into the afternoon/evening hours, with isolated supercells causing damage across the south-central mountains and across the southern tier during the evening hours. Steep lapse rates also allowed for supercells to produce large hail, with half-dollar sized hail in McAlevys Fort. Locally heavy rain from thunderstorms, coupled with saturated soils allowed for isolated instances of flooding to occur across Potter and Schuylkill counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to one-and-a-half inches of rainfall in one hour caused ponding of water on Route 67 in front of the Fairlane Village Mall near Pottsville, with one car becoming stranded and resulting in a single lane closure.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front located across southeastern Pennsylvania allowed for thunderstorms to begin forming during the afternoon hours of May 4, 2025. Ample moisture, with low LCL's and marginally sufficient 0-1 km shear values allowed for a brief tornado to form in East Cocalico Township, with peak winds estimated at 80 to 85 mph. The tornado destroyed three sheds and damaged the siding of two houses between Martzall Road and White Oak Road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service damage survey confirmed an EF0 tornado in East Cocalico Township on the afternoon of May 4, 2025, with peak winds estimated at 80 to 85 mph. The tornado destroyed three sheds and damaged the siding of two houses between Martzall Road and White Oak Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front stationed along the Allegheny Front and into south-central Pennsylvania provided ample lift in an unstable and highly sheared environment during the afternoon and evening hours of May 1, 2025. Scattered thunderstorms began to form during the late afternoon hour where peak heating led to destabilization along the Allegheny Front, with increased moisture pushed under southeasterly flow from the Chesapeake Bay. Numerous tree damage was reported from these thunderstorms in Clearfield, Centre, and Somerset counties with more widespread damage observed in Cambria County. In Cambria County, numerous trees were knocked down in Johnstown with more extensive damage in the vicinity of Chest Springs, where a one-story wood storage building was completely destroyed, along with a roof lifted off of a barn.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing wind gusts estimated at 60 mph knocked wires down near the intersection of Scullton Road and Hilltop Drive approximately 4 miles west-northwest of Casselman.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing wind gusts estimated at 70 mph knocked down numerous trees on the west and south side of Johnstown.
