EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front across northwestern Pennsylvania allowed for severe thunderstorms to form during the early morning hours across northwestern Pennsylvania, briefly producing quarter-sized hail before moving northeastward into New York. Thunderstorms associated with a cold front across Ohio and western Pennsylvania continued progressing further eastward, becoming more separated from the surface cold front as they progressed into central Pennsylvania during the afternoon hours of May 16, 2025. Enhanced destabilization across central Pennsylvania with ample shear in place allowed for thunderstorms to continue to maintain themselves as they continued into the afternoon/evening hours, with isolated supercells causing damage across the south-central mountains and across the southern tier during the evening hours. Steep lapse rates also allowed for supercells to produce large hail, with half-dollar sized hail in McAlevys Fort. Locally heavy rain from thunderstorms, coupled with saturated soils allowed for isolated instances of flooding to occur across Potter and Schuylkill counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing wind gusts estimated at 60 mph knocked a tree down on to Steigerwalt Hollow Road in York.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Destabilization ahead of a surface cold front stationed over Lake Erie, coupled with favorable shear and moisture in the warm sector allowed for an isolated severe thunderstorm to form across northwestern Pennsylvania during the afternoon hours of May 2, 2025. This severe thunderstorm continued progressing slowly to the east, into an environment with steep lapse rates and favorable buoyancy to produce hail across portions of McKean County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced quarter-sized hail in Bradford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure stationed across western Pennsylvania with a stationary front stationed across the southern tier allowed for widespread rain across the northern tier throughout the day on May 5, 2025. Ample low-level moisture, with relatively high precipitable water values (ranging between 1.00 and 1.50 inches), and robust moisture transport from the south allowed for this heavy rainfall to cause flooding in Tioga County, closing State Route 249 in Deerfield Township.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding was reported on PA Route 249 in Deerfield Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure stationed across the Delmarva during the early morning hours of May 6, 2025 began to lift north and east during the afternoon hours, allowing portions of eastern Pennsylvania to have a brief period of residence time in the warm sector. This residence time allowed for destabilization ahead of a surface cold front, with ample shear in place to allow for a line of showers and thunderstorms to slowly lift north and east through the region during the afternoon and evening hours. Severe thunderstorms associated with the line caused numerous tree damage in the Lower Susquehanna Valley, with a microburst producing a concentrated area of damage west of Orwigsburg consisting of snapped and uprooted trees. Heavy rain continued further north, where already saturated soils allowed for isolated instances of flooding closing roadways in Lycoming and Tioga counties. Heavy rainfall in Dauphin County also caused flash flooding, closing multiple roadways in Harrisburg during rush hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Approximately 2.50 of rain in an hour caused flash flooding along North Progress Avenue during the afternoon rush hour, leading to the closure of a lane of traffic due to water across the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Easterly flow, allowing for an influx of low-level moisture from the Chesapeake Bay, allowed for fog development across portions of eastern Pennsylvania during the early morning hours of June 17, 2025. Fog quickly became dense in the rugged terrain of Schuylkill County, allowing for an extended period of visibilities dropping near and below a quarter of a mile through the mid-morning hours of June 17th. Low visibility, due to fog, caused a motor vehicle accident involving three vehicles on the northbound lane of Interstate 81 causing one injury. A speed restriction was in place due to fog and low visibility on Interstate 81 at the time of the incident.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An extended period of dense fog was observed on Interstate 81 during the early morning hours of June 17, 2025. Low visibilities on the Interstate caused a three-vehicle incident, causing one injury and closing the roadway for an extended period of time. A speed restriction was in place for portions of Interstate 81 in Schuylkill County due to the low visibilities and fog.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed and moved across eastern Pennsylvania and northwestern New Jersey in response to a closed low north of our region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree was blown down and a pole was snapped in Bernville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread, long-lived thunderstorm line (bow echo) raced across Ohio and western Pennsylvania on the afternoon and early evening of 29 April 2025. The gusts associated with the line of storms reached above 80 MPH at times, producing widespread damage.||The thunderstorms which would eventually turn into the bow echo began to form over southern OH in the late morning hours. The storms became a line and began to bow out. The storms intensified as the bow moved across western PA in the afternoon. As it reached the Laurel Highlands, it was nearing it's peak intensity. An estimated 400,000 customers were without power in the Pittsburgh region as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell onto a powerline.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At the surface, an occluded low wobbled east-northeastward from southeastern ND to central Lake Superior during the early morning through late evening of May 16th. This caused a warm front to sweep northeastward across northwest PA during the early morning and then a cold front swept generally southeastward across northwest PA during the mid-morning. The cold front then retreated generally northward as a warm front across northwest PA during the mid to late evening of the 16th as a separate low along the front moved generally northeastward from the Lower MO Valley to southwestern Lower MI.||During the early morning of the 16th and in association with the first surface warm front, a line of multicell thunderstorms produced hail up to nickel-size and isolated straight-line wind damage in Erie County as the storms persisted generally eastward. The ambient environment exhibited moderate MUCAPE and moderate to strong effective bulk shear. Then, during the early evening of the 16th and ahead of the second surface warm front, a multicell thunderstorm generated isolated straight-line wind damage in southwestern Crawford County as the storm persisted northeastward amidst moderate effective bulk shear and weak to moderate MUCAPE.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree downed at PA-235 and South Canal Road in Conneaut Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving cold front was draped west to east across the southern Great Lakes during the day of June 18th, 2025, with low pressure near Chicago drifting east toward southern Ontario. An upper-level shortwave trough moving eastward across the southern Great Lakes during the afternoon brought scattered showers and thunderstorms to portions of northwest Pennsylvania, with slow-moving and back-building storms along a lake breeze front in far-northeast Erie County in Pennsylvania, which brought flash flooding. A quasi-linear convective system organized and strengthened across Illinois and Indiana during the afternoon and early evening hours and moved eastward across northern Ohio during the evening. This line of storms entered northwest Pennsylvania in the late evening and generated isolated straight-line wind damage in western Crawford County before weakening farther east. These storms traversed an ambient environment comprised of moderate to strong effective bulk shear and weak to moderate MUCAPE.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near the 8400 block of Gulf Road, a section of the roadway was washed out due to fast flowing water. Radar estimated 4 to 4.5 inches of rainfall in a 2 to 3 hour period, which roughly equates to a 500 year recurrence interval.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strongly forced band of convection developed over West Virginia in the morning ahead of an approaching front, and quickly lifted north-northeastward into Pennsylvania through the early afternoon. Despite relatively modest instability, very strong deep layer shear, along with largely unidirectional southerly flow and elongated hodographs, supported the continuation of significant severe storms. Large impact was felt from straight-line wind, with gusts in the 60 to 80 MPH range, locally higher, causing widespread damage to trees, power lines, and structures. The line of storms continued into Pennsylvania, where the environment was more favorable for tornadic development.  In addition to damaging winds, a number of large hail reports were received, with some stones as large as 1.75 inch diameter. Also, embedded supercells/mesovortices produced five confirmed tornadoes during the afternoon (one of which crossed from Fayette County into Westmoreland County).

The storms became a line and began to bow out. The storms intensified as the bow moved across western PA in the afternoon. As it reached the Laurel Highlands, it was nearing it's peak intensity. An estimated 400,000 customers were without power in the Pittsburgh region as a result.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving cold front was draped west to east across the southern Great Lakes during the day of June 18th, 2025, with low pressure near Chicago drifting east toward southern Ontario. An upper-level shortwave trough moving eastward across the southern Great Lakes during the afternoon brought scattered showers and thunderstorms to portions of northwest Pennsylvania, with slow-moving and back-building storms along a lake breeze front in far-northeast Erie County in Pennsylvania, which brought flash flooding. A quasi-linear convective system organized and strengthened across Illinois and Indiana during the afternoon and early evening hours and moved eastward across northern Ohio during the evening. This line of storms entered northwest Pennsylvania in the late evening and generated isolated straight-line wind damage in western Crawford County before weakening farther east. These storms traversed an ambient environment comprised of moderate to strong effective bulk shear and weak to moderate MUCAPE.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree downed in Springboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Prolonged dry conditions with low relative humidity values and gusty winds allowed for multiple wildfires to begin during the afternoon of April 23, 2025 in Michaux State Forest, located in southwestern Cumberland County. The wildfire originated along Shippensburg Road between Milesburn and Thompson Road and expanded quickly in size to around 2,248 acres at the wildfire's furthest extent. At the fire's peak, aerial support was enlisted to battle the fire, along with firefighting crews on the ground. Evacuations from structures began during the afternoon hours of April 24th and continued through April 25th with 20-30 residences evacuated. While the bulk of wildfire damage was contained in April 2025, wildfires continued into early May 2025. The Thompson Hollow and Hammond Rocks wildfires caused approximately $1,100,000 in tree damage along with $800,000 in suppression costs from the beginning of the wildfires in April 2025 through the early stages of May 2025.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire developed during the afternoon of April 23, 2025 in Michaux State Forest, located in southwestern Cumberland County. The wildfire originated along Shippensburg Road between Milesburn and Thompson Road. The wildfire occurred during a stretch of unseasonably dry weather along with gusty winds, and expanded quickly in size to around 2,248 acres at the wildfire's furthest extent. At the fire's peak, aerial support was enlisted to battle the fire, along with firefighting crews on the ground. Evacuations from structures began during the afternoon hours of April 24th and continued through April 25th with 20-30 residences evacuated. This wildfire also caused hazy/smokey conditions across Interstate 81 between mile markers 29 and 59 in both directions. Containment of the Thompson Hollow Wildfire began to increase late in the month but the fire continued to burn into the beginning of May 2025.||At the same time as the Thompson Hollow Wildfire, the Hammond Rocks Wildfire also ignited during the afternoon hours of April 24th, also burning 470 acres within the Michaux State Forest. Kings Gap State Park was evacuated due to the Hammond Rocks Wildfire, with aerial support also needed to contain the second wildfire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ample instability and modest shear ahead of an approaching cold front allowed for scattered convection to occur across the northern half of Pennsylvania during the early afternoon hours, producing isolated instances of tree and wire damage mainly along the Interstate 80 corridor of central Pennsylvania. Later in the evening hours, enhanced destabilization across the southern tier of Pennsylvania ahead of an approaching mesoscale convective system allowed for a long-track thunderstorm to traverse across the area, with widespread damage observed in a corridor of downdraft CAPE values approaching 1000 J/kg from Somerset County through Adams County, with sunset allowing for less destabilization further east. The most widespread damage was observed in Elk Lick Township and Addison Township in Somerset County, where straight-line wind damage brought down numerous trees and wires, with two people injured when their ATV collided with a large downed tree. Extensive damage was also observed in Salisbury (Somerset County) where a silo collapsed into a barn, completely destroying the barn.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm, producing estimated wind gusts near 70 mph, knocked down the awning of State Line Methodist Church in State Line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure stationed over western Quebec allowed for a surface cold front to track across central Pennsylvania alongside an upper-level jet streak right entrance region. Extremely favorable shear values orthogonal to the cold front with sufficient instability allowed for a quasi-liner convective system (QLCS) to form across western Pennsylvania and continue progressing across central Pennsylvania, causing widespread damage during the evening hours of April 29, 2025. The most significant damage was observed in/around State College, where multiple trees were knocked over onto residences and cars. In Centre County, one person was electrocuted due to downed power lines. Damage in Cambria County was also observed, where a barn collapse trapped hundreds of animals in Portage Township.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms producing wind gusts estimated at 70 mph blew a roof off of a building in the 6000 block of State Route 118. The roof knocked over a 1000 gallon propane tank as it blew off of the structure.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent shortwave moving east of the Great Lakes allowed for a frontal passage across central Pennsylvania, with marginal surface-based instability across the northern half of Pennsylvania. This allowed for a brief window for snow squall potential during the early morning hours of April 8, 2025. A snow squall produced a 30 car pileup in northern Centre County on Interstate 80, with the roadway between Kylertown and Snowshoe being closed for many hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. A snow squall occurred on the morning of April 8, 2025 and caused a 30 vehicle crash on Interstate 80. This resulted in the interstate being closed between Kylertown and Snow Shoe for much of the morning. Webcams showed snow rapidly accumulating on the roadway as the squall slowly moved through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper-level trough with enhanced flow and upper-level divergence in the wake of a surface cold front passage allowed for breezy to gusty winds to begin during the late morning and early afternoon hours of April 15, 2025. Ample moisture with sufficient lift and marginal instability allowed for numerous showers to form, producing gusty winds. As showers moved further south and east, elevated surface-based instability allowed for some thunderstorms to form, with damaging winds during the late afternoon hours. Shower coverage began to wane later in the day as the best forcing and upper-level flow shifted east of the region, with widespread damage across Centre and Dauphin Counties and isolated pockets of damage across eastern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm, producing estimated wind gusts near 60 mph, knocked down a tree and wires on Red Mill Road near Potters Mills, blocking the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper-level trough with enhanced flow and upper-level divergence in the wake of a surface cold front passage allowed for breezy to gusty winds to begin during the late morning and early afternoon hours of April 15, 2025. Ample moisture with sufficient lift and marginal instability allowed for numerous showers to form, producing gusty winds. As showers moved further south and east, elevated surface-based instability allowed for some thunderstorms to form, with damaging winds during the late afternoon hours. Shower coverage began to wane later in the day as the best forcing and upper-level flow shifted east of the region, with widespread damage across Centre and Dauphin Counties and isolated pockets of damage across eastern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm, producing estimated wind gusts near 60 mph, knocked down multiple trees west of Lebanon, with some instances of trees on wires.

This combination supported the development of mainly discrete storms over eastern Ohio into southwest PA, before storms began to congeal into clusters.  A few hail reports of up to 1.25 inch diameter were received, along with a number of wind damage reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A treed was downed on to Eddy Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface cold front traversed across central Pennsylvania during the evening hours of April 3rd, continuing into the early morning hours of April 4, 2025. This cold front brought ample lift with residual instability just ahead of the front with an increasing mid-level jet streak providing a marginally favorable environment for strong-to-severe thunderstorms. A severe thunderstorm formed across York County, producing a concentrated area of storm damage with multiple trees knocked down along with a trailer flipped in Sunnyburn.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm, producing estimated wind gusts near 60 mph, knocked down multiple trees on Thompson Road along Delta Road near Sunnyburn. On the 4500 block of Delta Road, a trailer was flipped with an outbuilding damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ample instability and modest shear ahead of an approaching cold front allowed for scattered convection to occur across the northern half of Pennsylvania during the early afternoon hours, producing isolated instances of tree and wire damage mainly along the Interstate 80 corridor of central Pennsylvania. Later in the evening hours, enhanced destabilization across the southern tier of Pennsylvania ahead of an approaching mesoscale convective system allowed for a long-track thunderstorm to traverse across the area, with widespread damage observed in a corridor of downdraft CAPE values approaching 1000 J/kg from Somerset County through Adams County, with sunset allowing for less destabilization further east. The most widespread damage was observed in Elk Lick Township and Addison Township in Somerset County, where straight-line wind damage brought down numerous trees and wires, with two people injured when their ATV collided with a large downed tree. Extensive damage was also observed in Salisbury (Somerset County) where a silo collapsed into a barn, completely destroying the barn.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced a measured wind gust of 64 mph at the Clearfield-Shawville Exit on Interstate 80.

The most significant damage was observed in/around State College, where multiple trees were knocked over onto residences and cars. In Centre County, one person was electrocuted due to downed power lines. Damage in Cambria County was also observed, where a barn collapse trapped hundreds of animals in Portage Township.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms producing wind gusts estimated at 70 mph knocked a tree down onto a car in Newry. No injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms producing wind gusts estimated at 70 mph knocked down a large tree and power lines near a residence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ample instability and modest shear ahead of an approaching cold front allowed for scattered convection to occur across the northern half of Pennsylvania during the early afternoon hours, producing isolated instances of tree and wire damage mainly along the Interstate 80 corridor of central Pennsylvania. Later in the evening hours, enhanced destabilization across the southern tier of Pennsylvania ahead of an approaching mesoscale convective system allowed for a long-track thunderstorm to traverse across the area, with widespread damage observed in a corridor of downdraft CAPE values approaching 1000 J/kg from Somerset County through Adams County, with sunset allowing for less destabilization further east. The most widespread damage was observed in Elk Lick Township and Addison Township in Somerset County, where straight-line wind damage brought down numerous trees and wires, with two people injured when their ATV collided with a large downed tree. Extensive damage was also observed in Salisbury (Somerset County) where a silo collapsed into a barn, completely destroying the barn.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm, producing estimated wind gusts near 60 mph, knocked down numerous trees in Clearfield.

In Centre County, one person was electrocuted due to downed power lines. Damage in Cambria County was also observed, where a barn collapse trapped hundreds of animals in Portage Township.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing wind gusts estimated at 60 mph knocked down multiple trees on to the 2800 block of Route 660 in Covington Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A triple point set up across central Pennsylvania and continued moving southeast during the evening hours of April 5, 2025 with elevated instability, ample shear, moisture, and lift in place across the southern tier of Pennsylvania. As showers and thunderstorms moved into the southern half of Pennsylvania, decreasing instability as sunset occurred led to strong thunderstorms beginning to weaken, with one instance of tree damage in Adams County as a tree fell onto Baltimore Pike.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm, producing estimated wind gusts near 50 mph, knocked a tree across the roadway on Baltimore Pike, closing it for a brief period of time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching warm front across west-central Pennsylvania moved along an axis of theta-e advection with sufficient shear and instability in place to promote thunderstorm development across western Pennsylvania. Increased mid-level lapse rates across the Laurel Highlands allowed for strong thunderstorms to continue producing sub-severe hail as they entered Cambria County before thunderstorm potential began to decrease rapidly as it quickly entered a more stable air mass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm produced penny-sized hail in Geistown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ample instability and modest shear ahead of an approaching cold front allowed for scattered convection to occur across the northern half of Pennsylvania during the early afternoon hours, producing isolated instances of tree and wire damage mainly along the Interstate 80 corridor of central Pennsylvania. Later in the evening hours, enhanced destabilization across the southern tier of Pennsylvania ahead of an approaching mesoscale convective system allowed for a long-track thunderstorm to traverse across the area, with widespread damage observed in a corridor of downdraft CAPE values approaching 1000 J/kg from Somerset County through Adams County, with sunset allowing for less destabilization further east. The most widespread damage was observed in Elk Lick Township and Addison Township in Somerset County, where straight-line wind damage brought down numerous trees and wires, with two people injured when their ATV collided with a large downed tree. Extensive damage was also observed in Salisbury (Somerset County) where a silo collapsed into a barn, completely destroying the barn.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm, producing estimated wind gusts near 70 mph, knocked down multiple trees near Rutledge Road in Mercersburg. This severe thunderstorm also produced damage to powerlines throughout Mercersburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper-level trough with enhanced flow and upper-level divergence in the wake of a surface cold front passage allowed for breezy to gusty winds to begin during the late morning and early afternoon hours of April 15, 2025. Ample moisture with sufficient lift and marginal instability allowed for numerous showers to form, producing gusty winds. As showers moved further south and east, elevated surface-based instability allowed for some thunderstorms to form, with damaging winds during the late afternoon hours. Shower coverage began to wane later in the day as the best forcing and upper-level flow shifted east of the region, with widespread damage across Centre and Dauphin Counties and isolated pockets of damage across eastern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced a measured wind gust of 58 mph at State College Regional Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system (MCS) dropped southward across Northeastern Pennsylvania moving into an environment with plenty of moisture and some instability during the late morning and early afternoon hours. While storms were non-severe, lightning was frequently observed. Two young men sustained minor injuries after being struck by lightning while working on a car.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two young men were injured after a lighting strike hit a tree and spread to them while they were working on a car in their driveway. They went to a local hospital to be checked out and were released after a few hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Diurnally driven showers and thunderstorms from the late evening hours of August 15, 2025 continued into the early morning hours of August 16th across eastern Pennsylvania. A non-severe thunderstorm drifted northward into Schuylkill County, producing efficient rainfall in a favorable moist environment, with gauge reports indicating between 3 and 6 inches of rainfall in a two-to-three hour timeframe. Flash flooding was observed in portions of Washington Township, with flooded roadways and basements. Diurnally driven showers and thunderstorms once again cropped up during the afternoon and evening hours of August 16, 2025 with locally heavy rainfall in Franklin County allowing for flooded roadways along the East Branch of Antietam Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from a slow-moving thunderstorm produced flash flooding in Waynesboro, with high water rising over roadways. Locations impacted included North Welty Road on the east side of town along the East Branch of Antietam Creek. Vehicles were stranded due to flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approaching from the Great Lakes during the morning hours of August 17, 2025 continued to advance into central Pennsylvania during the late morning to early afternoon hours. Ample forcing associated with the cold front coupled with sufficient instability, and a pocket of relatively dry air in the lower-levels allowed for isolated instances of damaging winds across the southern tier of Pennsylvania where shear remained marginally sufficient for thunderstorm development.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm, producing wind gusts estimated near 60 mph, knocked a tree down onto Glades Pike near Log House Road east of Somerset.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving along a stationary front triggered slow-moving, heavy rain-producing thunderstorms during the early morning hours. Pockets of 2 to 3 inches of rain fell over just a few hours causing urban and small stream flash flooding along with road and bridge washouts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flooding was reported at South Main Street and Quiet Cove.

Water was observed over multiple roadways in Washington Township with multiple residences reporting instances of basement flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface cold front stationed west of the region allowed for ample heating across the Susquehanna Valley during the afternoon and evening hours of August 13, 2025. Marginal shear and ample instability allowed for convection to begin firing, with strong-to-marginally severe thunderstorms to occur across southeastern Pennsylvania. An isolated instance of tree damage was observed in Lancaster County. Precipitable water values ranging generally between 1.50 and 2.00 allowed for efficient rainfall in thunderstorms across York and Lancaster Counties, with a basement taking on water in southern York County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm, producing wind gusts estimated near 60 mph, knocked a tree down onto the roadway near the intersection of Butter Road and Hunsicker Road north of Zooks Corner.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure stationed over the Great Lakes to southwestern New York State allowed for an occluded front to drape across northwestern Pennsylvania during the early afternoon hours of August 20, 2025. Multiple reports of a funnel cloud were observed near Youngsville in Warren County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud formed near the intersection of US Route 6 and US Route 62 east of Youngsville.  The funnel cloud was confirmed via numerous videos from Facebook along with a report from the Director of Warren County EMA.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure associated with a seasonably anomalous closed upper low dragged a cold front through the area, resulting in evening showers and thunderstorms. Most convection was sub-severe as MLCAPE and effective shear were marginal (300-600 J/g and 20kts), however, there was one severe storm east of Pittsburgh in Westmoreland county. Damaging winds were the only threat with convection.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down along Old Distillery Road at Foggy Mountain Lodge.

These storms drifted over highly urbanized areas and produced excessive rainfall and minor street and poor drainage flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Road closed due to high water near Mulberry and Prescott Ave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system (MCS) dropped southward across Central NY from eastern Ontario, moving into an environment with plenty of moisture and instability during the early morning hours. Torrential rainfall accompanied these storms and produced widespread flash flooding and small river flooding. Estimates of 3 to 5 inches, with locally higher amounts, fell mostly within a 6-hour period and affected portions of Northeast Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was flooding Interstate 81 near Great Bend.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving surface warm front stationed across western Pennsylvania allowed for slow-moving showers and thunderstorms to form across southwestern Pennsylvania in an environment of anomalously moist air with long, skinny CAPE and enhanced low-level shear. Showers and thunderstorms across southwestern Pennsylvania initially formed during the early afternoon hours and continued into the evening hours, producing numerous instances of flash flooding and closing roadways across Cambria, Blair, Bedford, and Fulton counties. The aforementioned low-level shear and instability allowed for a brief EF1 tornado to occur across southeastern Bedford County, on York Road. The maximum wind speed of the York Road Tornado was 95 mph, with a 1.1-mile path and a width of 175 yards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The basement of a home on Lakeview Road filled with water and water was approaching first floor of the home.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was observed on Cardiff Road near Railroad Street on the north side of  Nanty-Glo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving surface warm front stationed across western Pennsylvania allowed for slow-moving showers and thunderstorms to form across southwestern Pennsylvania in an environment of anomalously moist air with long, skinny CAPE and enhanced low-level shear. Showers and thunderstorms across southwestern Pennsylvania initially formed during the early afternoon hours and continued into the evening hours, producing numerous instances of flash flooding and closing roadways across Cambria, Blair, Bedford, and Fulton counties. The aforementioned low-level shear and instability allowed for a brief EF1 tornado to occur across southeastern Bedford County, on York Road. The maximum wind speed of the York Road Tornado was 95 mph, with a 1.1-mile path and a width of 175 yards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding resulted in four feet of water across the intersection of South Center Street and Lakeview Road in Ebensburg, closing the intersection.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously strong upper-level ridge began to set up across the eastern contiguous United States, allowing for an extended period of heat across portions of Central Pennsylvania beginning on June 22, 2025. High pressure building to the south on June 23, 2025 allowed for the hottest temperatures and heat index values to occur during the afternoon hours of June 23rd and June 24th across portions of the Susquehanna Valley, highest across the southern tier of Pennsylvania. High pressure began to slowly break down later on in the day of June 24th, allowing for slightly cooling temperatures with an approaching low pressure system from the Ohio Valley allowing for further cooling by June 25, 2025.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Well-above average temperatures, coupled with humid conditions, allowed for multiple days of heat index values over 100 degrees beginning during the afternoon hours of June 22nd and continuing through the late evening hours of June 25, 2025 across Perry County. Heat index values topped out between 105 and 107 degrees during the afternoon and evening hours of June 23, 2025. Warm and humid conditions continued into the overnight hours throughout this period, offering little relief throughout the time period. Multiple cooling stations were opened throughout the county during this stretch of abnormally cool temperatures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving warm front in an extremely moist (PWAT values exceeding 2.00, values closer to 2.25 across the Lower Susquehanna Valley) and unstable environment allowed for multiple clusters of thunderstorms to begin to form during the late morning hours and continue through the late evening hours of June 30, 2025. A shortwave trough stationed across the region allowed for enhanced lift, with relatively weak shear in place, bringing a severe threat in the form of wet downbursts and a flash flood threat given training thunderstorms with efficient rainfall rates in excess of two inches per hour across southern Pennsylvania. Isolated thunderstorm wind damage was observed across Tioga County, with tree damage observed in Lancaster County and a widespread area of trees and wires knocked down in Cumberland County due to a wet microburst. Multiple rounds of thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours across Lancaster County, where multiple days of heavy rainfall led to low flash flood guidance, prompted widespread flash flooding across the northern half of the county, with multiple water rescues reported in Mount Joy and Manheim. Multiple roadways were closed across northern Lancaster County and southern Lebanon County due to heavy rain, with isolated instances of flash flooding in Cumberland County. Flooding concerns in Lancaster County continued into the early morning hours of July 1, 2025 as the heaviest rain began to move out of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm, producing winds estimated near 60 mph, knocked down multiple trees down onto Weigand Boulevard in Port Carbon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold frontal passage during the evening hours of June 19, 2025 brought a broken line of showers and thunderstorms to central Pennsylvania in a moderately sheared and unstable environment. Ahead of the cold front, unstable conditions with ample moisture and shear in place allowed for multiple clusters of severe thunderstorms to form along the western mountains, and continue eastward as the evening progressed. One of these clusters moved northward from eastern Maryland into York and Lancaster counties, producing multiple instances of tree damage across southern portions of each county. A cluster of thunderstorms across Clearfield and Centre counties produced multiple instances of tree damage before continuing to cause isolated tree damage into the Middle Susquehanna Valley. The most widespread damage was observed in eastern Union County and northern Northumberland County, where more thunderstorms began to bow out and produce widespread straight-line wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms producing wind gusts estimated at 60 mph knocked several trees down onto cars in Lewisburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface cold front stationed across northern Pennsylvania allowed for scattered showers and thunderstorms to form in an environment with ample instability and limited shear during the afternoon and evening hours of June 25, 2025. These slow-moving showers and thunderstorms quickly became strong-to-severe with the aforementioned instability and were able to sustain and produce isolated instances of thunderstorm wind damage across Schuylkill and Clinton counties. Ample moisture in place, allowed for heavy rain with these showers and thunderstorms with water over roadways in urban areas of Centre County. A couple of lightning strikes from thunderstorms also caused structure fires during the evening, with one near Lock Haven and the other northwest of McConnellsburg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm, producing wind gusts estimated near 60 mph knocked down a tree on Church Road near New Ringgold.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An organized line of convection crossed the region, with 40-45 knots of unidirectional shear out of the southwest. This along with 100-1500 J/kg of surface-based CAPE resulted in severe weather across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Received a picture of hail next to a ruler from an EM near Sligo, PA.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold frontal passage during the evening hours of June 19, 2025 brought a broken line of showers and thunderstorms to central Pennsylvania in a moderately sheared and unstable environment. Ahead of the cold front, unstable conditions with ample moisture and shear in place allowed for multiple clusters of severe thunderstorms to form along the western mountains, and continue eastward as the evening progressed. One of these clusters moved northward from eastern Maryland into York and Lancaster counties, producing multiple instances of tree damage across southern portions of each county. A cluster of thunderstorms across Clearfield and Centre counties produced multiple instances of tree damage before continuing to cause isolated tree damage into the Middle Susquehanna Valley. The most widespread damage was observed in eastern Union County and northern Northumberland County, where more thunderstorms began to bow out and produce widespread straight-line wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms producing wind gusts estimated at 60 mph knocked multiple trees onto wires approximately 3 miles north-northeast of Bloomsburg. One of the trees caught fire.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm, producing wind gusts estimated near 70 mph, knocked down and uprooted multiple trees in Milton. Shingles were also blown off on some residences in Milton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface cold front moved across northwestern Pennsylvania during the afternoon and began to fire showers and isolated thunderstorms given modest instability and sufficient moisture and shear. Thunderstorms across northwestern Pennsylvania gradually continued to become stronger and borderline severe as they entered the best moisture and instability of northwestern Pennsylvania, allowing for one complex of thunderstorms to cause tree damage in Warren and McKean counties during the afternoon and evening hours of June 5, 2025.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm, producing wind gusts estimated near 60 mph, knocked down a tree down on Route 62 near the intersection of Bimber Run Road, blocking the roadway east of Tidioute.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving surface warm front stationed across western Pennsylvania allowed for slow-moving showers and thunderstorms to form across southwestern Pennsylvania in an environment of anomalously moist air with long, skinny CAPE and enhanced low-level shear. Showers and thunderstorms across southwestern Pennsylvania initially formed during the early afternoon hours and continued into the evening hours, producing numerous instances of flash flooding and closing roadways across Cambria, Blair, Bedford, and Fulton counties. The aforementioned low-level shear and instability allowed for a brief EF1 tornado to occur across southeastern Bedford County, on York Road. The maximum wind speed of the York Road Tornado was 95 mph, with a 1.1-mile path and a width of 175 yards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and flash flooding caused a rock slide near Town Hill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously strong upper-level ridge began to set up across the eastern contiguous United States, allowing for an extended period of heat across portions of Central Pennsylvania beginning on June 22, 2025. High pressure building to the south on June 23, 2025 allowed for the hottest temperatures and heat index values to occur during the afternoon hours of June 23rd and June 24th across portions of the Susquehanna Valley, highest across the southern tier of Pennsylvania. High pressure began to slowly break down later on in the day of June 24th, allowing for slightly cooling temperatures with an approaching low pressure system from the Ohio Valley allowing for further cooling by June 25, 2025.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Well-above average temperatures, coupled with humid conditions, allowed for multiple days of heat index values over 100 degrees beginning during the afternoon hours of June 22nd and continuing through the late evening hours of June 25, 2025 across Columbia County. Heat index values topped out between 105 and 107 degrees during the afternoon and evening hours of June 23, 2025. Warm and humid conditions continued into the overnight hours throughout this period, offering little relief throughout the time period. Multiple cooling stations were opened throughout the county during this stretch of abnormally cool temperatures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving warm front in an extremely moist (PWAT values exceeding 2.00, values closer to 2.25 across the Lower Susquehanna Valley) and unstable environment allowed for multiple clusters of thunderstorms to begin to form during the late morning hours and continue through the late evening hours of June 30, 2025. A shortwave trough stationed across the region allowed for enhanced lift, with relatively weak shear in place, bringing a severe threat in the form of wet downbursts and a flash flood threat given training thunderstorms with efficient rainfall rates in excess of two inches per hour across southern Pennsylvania. Isolated thunderstorm wind damage was observed across Tioga County, with tree damage observed in Lancaster County and a widespread area of trees and wires knocked down in Cumberland County due to a wet microburst. Multiple rounds of thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours across Lancaster County, where multiple days of heavy rainfall led to low flash flood guidance, prompted widespread flash flooding across the northern half of the county, with multiple water rescues reported in Mount Joy and Manheim. Multiple roadways were closed across northern Lancaster County and southern Lebanon County due to heavy rain, with isolated instances of flash flooding in Cumberland County. Flooding concerns in Lancaster County continued into the early morning hours of July 1, 2025 as the heaviest rain began to move out of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm produced penny-sized hail near Highville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface cold front stationed across northern Pennsylvania allowed for scattered showers and thunderstorms to form in an environment with ample instability and limited shear during the afternoon and evening hours of June 25, 2025. These slow-moving showers and thunderstorms quickly became strong-to-severe with the aforementioned instability and were able to sustain and produce isolated instances of thunderstorm wind damage across Schuylkill and Clinton counties. Ample moisture in place, allowed for heavy rain with these showers and thunderstorms with water over roadways in urban areas of Centre County. A couple of lightning strikes from thunderstorms also caused structure fires during the evening, with one near Lock Haven and the other northwest of McConnellsburg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a residence in Licking Creek Township, causing a house fire. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short-wave trough moving into central Pennsylvania in a sufficiently unstable and sheared environment allowed for numerous showers to occur during the early afternoon hours of June 18, 2025 with thunderstorm coverage increasing during the mid-afternoon hours and into the evening. Lake breeze convection across northwestern Pennsylvania allowed for scattered thunderstorms to move into the sheared and unstable environment with a line of showers and isolated severe thunderstorms progressing across the northern half of central Pennsylvania. Early afternoon showers produced efficient rainfall, causing an instance of flash flooding in northern Clearfield County, with a roadway closed due to high water. Damage with the initial lake breeze convection was limited to Warren and Elk counties in the form of tree damage. Severe thunderstorms with the line of thunderstorms produced isolated instances of tree damage along the Interstate 80 corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm, producing wind gusts estimated at 60 mph, knocked down a tree into wires on Davey Hill Road northeast of Tidioute.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system stationed along the Hudson Bay allowed for central Pennsylvania to experience a brief period in the warm sector during the afternoon hours of June 9, 2025 ahead of a mid-level trough. This trough aided in large-scale ascent ahead of a surface cold front that aided in supercell development across western and central Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening with the trailing cold front, allowing for a second round of thunderstorms containing locally heavy rainfall and some severe thunderstorms that produced wind damage. Locally heavy rainfall in Altoona led to multiple roadways in and around the city becoming impassable with States Road quickly being washed out by the rainfall. Later in the evening, two supercells formed across the central mountains of Pennsylvania, with the southernmost one producing large hail in Mifflin County and producing widespread tree damage in northern Huntingdon County. The northernmost supercell produced multiple trees and wires down in Milesburg. An isolated severe thunderstorm also knocked down two trees into a residence in northern Perry County, with no injuries reported. Heavy rainfall also caused isolated instances of flash flooding in central Bedford County and northern Huntingdon County, with flooding reported in Perry County during the early morning hours of June 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced half-dollar sized hail in Lumber City.

Lake breeze convection across northwestern Pennsylvania allowed for scattered thunderstorms to move into the sheared and unstable environment with a line of showers and isolated severe thunderstorms progressing across the northern half of central Pennsylvania. Early afternoon showers produced efficient rainfall, causing an instance of flash flooding in northern Clearfield County, with a roadway closed due to high water. Damage with the initial lake breeze convection was limited to Warren and Elk counties in the form of tree damage. Severe thunderstorms with the line of thunderstorms produced isolated instances of tree damage along the Interstate 80 corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm, producing winds estimated near 60 mph, knocked a tree down on wires along Calabash Court southeast of Treasure Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously strong upper-level ridge began to set up across the eastern contiguous United States, allowing for an extended period of heat across portions of Central Pennsylvania beginning on June 22, 2025. High pressure building to the south on June 23, 2025 allowed for the hottest temperatures and heat index values to occur during the afternoon hours of June 23rd and June 24th across portions of the Susquehanna Valley, highest across the southern tier of Pennsylvania. High pressure began to slowly break down later on in the day of June 24th, allowing for slightly cooling temperatures with an approaching low pressure system from the Ohio Valley allowing for further cooling by June 25, 2025.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Well-above average temperatures, coupled with humid conditions, allowed for multiple days of heat index values between 100 and 105 degrees beginning during the afternoon hours of June 23 and continuing through the late evening hours of June 25, 2025 across southern Centre County. Warm and humid conditions continued into the overnight hours throughout this period, offering little relief throughout the time period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving warm front in an extremely moist (PWAT values exceeding 2.00, values closer to 2.25 across the Lower Susquehanna Valley) and unstable environment allowed for multiple clusters of thunderstorms to begin to form during the late morning hours and continue through the late evening hours of June 30, 2025. A shortwave trough stationed across the region allowed for enhanced lift, with relatively weak shear in place, bringing a severe threat in the form of wet downbursts and a flash flood threat given training thunderstorms with efficient rainfall rates in excess of two inches per hour across southern Pennsylvania. Isolated thunderstorm wind damage was observed across Tioga County, with tree damage observed in Lancaster County and a widespread area of trees and wires knocked down in Cumberland County due to a wet microburst. Multiple rounds of thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours across Lancaster County, where multiple days of heavy rainfall led to low flash flood guidance, prompted widespread flash flooding across the northern half of the county, with multiple water rescues reported in Mount Joy and Manheim. Multiple roadways were closed across northern Lancaster County and southern Lebanon County due to heavy rain, with isolated instances of flash flooding in Cumberland County. Flooding concerns in Lancaster County continued into the early morning hours of July 1, 2025 as the heaviest rain began to move out of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing wind gusts estimated at 60 mph knocked multiple trees down in Pottsville. One tree fell onto a car on Fairmount Avenue. No injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down along Mercer Road and partially blocking a lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system stationed along the Hudson Bay allowed for central Pennsylvania to experience a brief period in the warm sector during the afternoon hours of June 9, 2025 ahead of a mid-level trough. This trough aided in large-scale ascent ahead of a surface cold front that aided in supercell development across western and central Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening with the trailing cold front, allowing for a second round of thunderstorms containing locally heavy rainfall and some severe thunderstorms that produced wind damage. Locally heavy rainfall in Altoona led to multiple roadways in and around the city becoming impassable with States Road quickly being washed out by the rainfall. Later in the evening, two supercells formed across the central mountains of Pennsylvania, with the southernmost one producing large hail in Mifflin County and producing widespread tree damage in northern Huntingdon County. The northernmost supercell produced multiple trees and wires down in Milesburg. An isolated severe thunderstorm also knocked down two trees into a residence in northern Perry County, with no injuries reported. Heavy rainfall also caused isolated instances of flash flooding in central Bedford County and northern Huntingdon County, with flooding reported in Perry County during the early morning hours of June 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm, producing wind gusts estimated near 60 mph, knocked down a tree down on Bellemeade Drive near the intersection of Linwood Road, blocking the roadway north of Altoona.

Multiple cooling stations were opened throughout the county during this stretch of abnormally cool temperatures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving warm front in an extremely moist (PWAT values exceeding 2.00, values closer to 2.25 across the Lower Susquehanna Valley) and unstable environment allowed for multiple clusters of thunderstorms to begin to form during the late morning hours and continue through the late evening hours of June 30, 2025. A shortwave trough stationed across the region allowed for enhanced lift, with relatively weak shear in place, bringing a severe threat in the form of wet downbursts and a flash flood threat given training thunderstorms with efficient rainfall rates in excess of two inches per hour across southern Pennsylvania. Isolated thunderstorm wind damage was observed across Tioga County, with tree damage observed in Lancaster County and a widespread area of trees and wires knocked down in Cumberland County due to a wet microburst. Multiple rounds of thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours across Lancaster County, where multiple days of heavy rainfall led to low flash flood guidance, prompted widespread flash flooding across the northern half of the county, with multiple water rescues reported in Mount Joy and Manheim. Multiple roadways were closed across northern Lancaster County and southern Lebanon County due to heavy rain, with isolated instances of flash flooding in Cumberland County. Flooding concerns in Lancaster County continued into the early morning hours of July 1, 2025 as the heaviest rain began to move out of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing wind gusts estimated at 70 mph knocked a tree onto a house on Courtyard Drive north of Carlisle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An organized line of convection crossed the region, with 40-45 knots of unidirectional shear out of the southwest. This along with 100-1500 J/kg of surface-based CAPE resulted in severe weather across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mature mesocyclone developed over Clarion County at roughly 5pm|on June 9th, 2025. Mid level rotation intensified as the storm|grew stronger, until eventually, a brief tornado touched down near|Bush Run Road in Waterson, PA in Clarion County. Most damage was|to hardwood trees. One tree fell on a garage, collapsing the|structure. Winds were observed in a narrow swath and trees fell in|a convergent pattern for roughly a quarter of a mile. The damage|was consistent with a brief EF0 tornado lasting only a couple of|minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strongly forced band of convection developed over West Virginia in the morning ahead of an approaching front, and quickly lifted north-northeastward into Pennsylvania through the early afternoon. Despite relatively modest instability, very strong deep layer shear, along with largely unidirectional southerly flow and elongated hodographs, supported the continuation of significant severe storms. Large impact was felt from straight-line wind, with gusts in the 60 to 80 MPH range, locally higher, causing widespread damage to trees, power lines, and structures. The line of storms continued into Pennsylvania, where the environment was more favorable for tornadic development.  In addition to damaging winds, a number of large hail reports were received, with some stones as large as 1.75 inch diameter. Also, embedded supercells/mesovortices produced five confirmed tornadoes during the afternoon (one of which crossed from Fayette County into Westmoreland County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A team from the National Weather Service office in Pittsburgh performed a storm survey along a pair of storm tracks associated with mesovortex structures in the line of severe thunderstorms that moved through the region Sunday afternoon, March 16th. One area was near Dilliner, along the shore of the Monongahela River, where the mesovortex interacted with terrain to create a larger area of  tree damage, estimated to have been caused by 80 MPH wind. The concentrated swath of damage was located on the southern-end surge of a meso-vortex embedded in a linear convective system with widespread winds of 55mph to 75mph along Cemetery Hill Road, with some acceleration to as much as 80mph as the wind accelerated into the Monongahela Valley with terrain influence. After further examination, including the evaluation of high-resolution satellite data, it was determined that the damage was non-tornadic.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold frontal passage during the evening hours of June 19, 2025 brought a broken line of showers and thunderstorms to central Pennsylvania in a moderately sheared and unstable environment. Ahead of the cold front, unstable conditions with ample moisture and shear in place allowed for multiple clusters of severe thunderstorms to form along the western mountains, and continue eastward as the evening progressed. One of these clusters moved northward from eastern Maryland into York and Lancaster counties, producing multiple instances of tree damage across southern portions of each county. A cluster of thunderstorms across Clearfield and Centre counties produced multiple instances of tree damage before continuing to cause isolated tree damage into the Middle Susquehanna Valley. The most widespread damage was observed in eastern Union County and northern Northumberland County, where more thunderstorms began to bow out and produce widespread straight-line wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms producing wind gusts estimated at 60 mph knocked several trees and power lines down near Conestoga.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moving across central Pennsylvania during the morning hours of June 28, 2025 with a surface cold front stationed in the Great Lake region allowed for much of central Pennsylvania to reside in the warm sector during the early afternoon and evening hours. Increasing instability, shear, and moisture due to this residence time primed the atmosphere ahead of a prefrontal trough allowing for ample lift to produce showers and thunderstorms. Thunderstorms in western Pennsylvania during the late morning hours quickly grew strong-to-severe, producing an isolated instance of tree damage in Elk County, continuing eastward and producing damage in southern Clinton and southern Lycoming counties. Scattered thunderstorms also began to form across southern Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening hours with slow-moving, severe thunderstorms producing large hail, tree damage, and flash flooding across Lancaster County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced 1.25 inch hail approximately 1 mile northwest of Mountville.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a house near the intersection of Hahnstown Road and Glenwood Drive in Ephrata Township. No report of injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system stationed along the Hudson Bay allowed for central Pennsylvania to experience a brief period in the warm sector during the afternoon hours of June 9, 2025 ahead of a mid-level trough. This trough aided in large-scale ascent ahead of a surface cold front that aided in supercell development across western and central Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening with the trailing cold front, allowing for a second round of thunderstorms containing locally heavy rainfall and some severe thunderstorms that produced wind damage. Locally heavy rainfall in Altoona led to multiple roadways in and around the city becoming impassable with States Road quickly being washed out by the rainfall. Later in the evening, two supercells formed across the central mountains of Pennsylvania, with the southernmost one producing large hail in Mifflin County and producing widespread tree damage in northern Huntingdon County. The northernmost supercell produced multiple trees and wires down in Milesburg. An isolated severe thunderstorm also knocked down two trees into a residence in northern Perry County, with no injuries reported. Heavy rainfall also caused isolated instances of flash flooding in central Bedford County and northern Huntingdon County, with flooding reported in Perry County during the early morning hours of June 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm, producing wind gusts estimated near 95 mph, uprooted and snapped numerous trees in Rothrock State Forest. The most concentrated damage as a result of this downburst snapped three to four dozen trees in a densely forested area, with a combination of softwood pines and hardwood oaks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short-wave trough moving into central Pennsylvania in a sufficiently unstable and sheared environment allowed for numerous showers to occur during the early afternoon hours of June 18, 2025 with thunderstorm coverage increasing during the mid-afternoon hours and into the evening. Lake breeze convection across northwestern Pennsylvania allowed for scattered thunderstorms to move into the sheared and unstable environment with a line of showers and isolated severe thunderstorms progressing across the northern half of central Pennsylvania. Early afternoon showers produced efficient rainfall, causing an instance of flash flooding in northern Clearfield County, with a roadway closed due to high water. Damage with the initial lake breeze convection was limited to Warren and Elk counties in the form of tree damage. Severe thunderstorms with the line of thunderstorms produced isolated instances of tree damage along the Interstate 80 corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm, producing wind gusts estimated near 60 mph, knocked down a tree and wires onto German Road in Woolrich.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously strong upper-level ridge began to set up across the eastern contiguous United States, allowing for an extended period of heat across portions of Central Pennsylvania beginning on June 22, 2025. High pressure building to the south on June 23, 2025 allowed for the hottest temperatures and heat index values to occur during the afternoon hours of June 23rd and June 24th across portions of the Susquehanna Valley, highest across the southern tier of Pennsylvania. High pressure began to slowly break down later on in the day of June 24th, allowing for slightly cooling temperatures with an approaching low pressure system from the Ohio Valley allowing for further cooling by June 25, 2025.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Well-above average temperatures, coupled with humid conditions, allowed for multiple days of heat index values between 100 and 105 degrees beginning during the afternoon hours of June 23 and continuing through the late evening hours of June 25, 2025 across northern Clinton County. Warm and humid conditions continued into the overnight hours throughout this period, offering little relief throughout the time period. Multiple cooling stations were opened throughout the county during this stretch of abnormally cool temperatures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and humid conditions under the influence of a lee-side trough allowed for scattered thunderstorm development during the afternoon and evening hours of June 26, 2025. Ample moisture in place across the region (PWATs in 1.75 to 2.00 range), coupled with overly favorable instability (CAPE in the 2000-3000 J/kg range) allowed for pulse thunderstorms to push up towards severe limits and trend downward without sufficient shear in place across central Pennsylvania. Severe thunderstorms across south-central and southeastern Pennsylvania produced scattered instances of tree damage. This lack of shear also allowed for thunderstorms, producing efficient rainfall rates exceeding one inch per hour, to stall out and/or move slowly, prompting isolated instances of flash flooding across Lycoming County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing wind gusts estimated at 60 mph knocked multiple trees down in the area of N Clear Ridge Road in Dublin Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front set up across the Laurel Highlands during the morning and afternoon hours of June 6, 2025 providing ample lift in an environment with plentiful moisture and sufficient instability to produce a severe thunderstorm in Cambria County, with multiple trees down in Johnstown. As the day continued, the stationary front took on more warm frontal characteristics, providing a favorable environment for heavy rainfall across central Pennsylvania. This heavy rainfall caused an isolated instance of flooding in Clinton County with flooding in front of the Bald Eagle Township Building closing Fairpoint Road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm, producing winds gusts estimated at 60 mph, knocked down multiple trees in the Johnstown area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of thunderstorms trained over portions of Lawrence, Allegheny, Westmoreland Clarion and Beaver counties on June 6th. This was a result of a very moist airmass and a stationary boundary located over northern Ohio and northwestern Pennsylvania. Flash flooding was reported in the aforementioned counties with considerable flooding noted in Allegheny county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State official reported flooding on PA 108 in both directions between Shiderly Lane and Old Hickory Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold frontal passage during the evening hours of June 19, 2025 brought a broken line of showers and thunderstorms to central Pennsylvania in a moderately sheared and unstable environment. Ahead of the cold front, unstable conditions with ample moisture and shear in place allowed for multiple clusters of severe thunderstorms to form along the western mountains, and continue eastward as the evening progressed. One of these clusters moved northward from eastern Maryland into York and Lancaster counties, producing multiple instances of tree damage across southern portions of each county. A cluster of thunderstorms across Clearfield and Centre counties produced multiple instances of tree damage before continuing to cause isolated tree damage into the Middle Susquehanna Valley. The most widespread damage was observed in eastern Union County and northern Northumberland County, where more thunderstorms began to bow out and produce widespread straight-line wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms producing wind gusts estimated at 60 mph uprooted multiple large trees in Delta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moving across central Pennsylvania during the morning hours of June 28, 2025 with a surface cold front stationed in the Great Lake region allowed for much of central Pennsylvania to reside in the warm sector during the early afternoon and evening hours. Increasing instability, shear, and moisture due to this residence time primed the atmosphere ahead of a prefrontal trough allowing for ample lift to produce showers and thunderstorms. Thunderstorms in western Pennsylvania during the late morning hours quickly grew strong-to-severe, producing an isolated instance of tree damage in Elk County, continuing eastward and producing damage in southern Clinton and southern Lycoming counties. Scattered thunderstorms also began to form across southern Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening hours with slow-moving, severe thunderstorms producing large hail, tree damage, and flash flooding across Lancaster County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing wind gusts estimated at 60 mph knocked down multiple trees and power lines in Loganton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system stationed along the Hudson Bay allowed for central Pennsylvania to experience a brief period in the warm sector during the afternoon hours of June 9, 2025 ahead of a mid-level trough. This trough aided in large-scale ascent ahead of a surface cold front that aided in supercell development across western and central Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening with the trailing cold front, allowing for a second round of thunderstorms containing locally heavy rainfall and some severe thunderstorms that produced wind damage. Locally heavy rainfall in Altoona led to multiple roadways in and around the city becoming impassable with States Road quickly being washed out by the rainfall. Later in the evening, two supercells formed across the central mountains of Pennsylvania, with the southernmost one producing large hail in Mifflin County and producing widespread tree damage in northern Huntingdon County. The northernmost supercell produced multiple trees and wires down in Milesburg. An isolated severe thunderstorm also knocked down two trees into a residence in northern Perry County, with no injuries reported. Heavy rainfall also caused isolated instances of flash flooding in central Bedford County and northern Huntingdon County, with flooding reported in Perry County during the early morning hours of June 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of flash flooding, flooded roadways, and road closures in Altoona were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short-wave trough moving into central Pennsylvania in a sufficiently unstable and sheared environment allowed for numerous showers to occur during the early afternoon hours of June 18, 2025 with thunderstorm coverage increasing during the mid-afternoon hours and into the evening. Lake breeze convection across northwestern Pennsylvania allowed for scattered thunderstorms to move into the sheared and unstable environment with a line of showers and isolated severe thunderstorms progressing across the northern half of central Pennsylvania. Early afternoon showers produced efficient rainfall, causing an instance of flash flooding in northern Clearfield County, with a roadway closed due to high water. Damage with the initial lake breeze convection was limited to Warren and Elk counties in the form of tree damage. Severe thunderstorms with the line of thunderstorms produced isolated instances of tree damage along the Interstate 80 corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding led to water covering Flowing Well Road at Coal Run to the east of Dubois.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously strong upper-level ridge began to set up across the eastern contiguous United States, allowing for an extended period of heat across portions of Central Pennsylvania beginning on June 22, 2025. High pressure building to the south on June 23, 2025 allowed for the hottest temperatures and heat index values to occur during the afternoon hours of June 23rd and June 24th across portions of the Susquehanna Valley, highest across the southern tier of Pennsylvania. High pressure began to slowly break down later on in the day of June 24th, allowing for slightly cooling temperatures with an approaching low pressure system from the Ohio Valley allowing for further cooling by June 25, 2025.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Well-above average temperatures, coupled with humid conditions, allowed for multiple days of heat index values over 100 degrees beginning during the afternoon hours of June 22nd and continuing through the late evening hours of June 25, 2025 across Lancaster County. Heat index values topped out between 105 and 107 degrees during the afternoon and evening hours of June 23 and June 24, 2025. Warm and humid conditions continued into the overnight hours throughout this period, offering little relief throughout the time period. Multiple cooling centers and water stations were opened throughout the county during this stretch of abnormally cool temperatures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and humid conditions under the influence of a lee-side trough allowed for scattered thunderstorm development during the afternoon and evening hours of June 26, 2025. Ample moisture in place across the region (PWATs in 1.75 to 2.00 range), coupled with overly favorable instability (CAPE in the 2000-3000 J/kg range) allowed for pulse thunderstorms to push up towards severe limits and trend downward without sufficient shear in place across central Pennsylvania. Severe thunderstorms across south-central and southeastern Pennsylvania produced scattered instances of tree damage. This lack of shear also allowed for thunderstorms, producing efficient rainfall rates exceeding one inch per hour, to stall out and/or move slowly, prompting isolated instances of flash flooding across Lycoming County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing wind gusts estimated at 60 mph knocked a tree down onto South Jefferson Street in Mount Union which blocked the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving warm front in an extremely moist (PWAT values exceeding 2.00, values closer to 2.25 across the Lower Susquehanna Valley) and unstable environment allowed for multiple clusters of thunderstorms to begin to form during the late morning hours and continue through the late evening hours of June 30, 2025. A shortwave trough stationed across the region allowed for enhanced lift, with relatively weak shear in place, bringing a severe threat in the form of wet downbursts and a flash flood threat given training thunderstorms with efficient rainfall rates in excess of two inches per hour across southern Pennsylvania. Isolated thunderstorm wind damage was observed across Tioga County, with tree damage observed in Lancaster County and a widespread area of trees and wires knocked down in Cumberland County due to a wet microburst. Multiple rounds of thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours across Lancaster County, where multiple days of heavy rainfall led to low flash flood guidance, prompted widespread flash flooding across the northern half of the county, with multiple water rescues reported in Mount Joy and Manheim. Multiple roadways were closed across northern Lancaster County and southern Lebanon County due to heavy rain, with isolated instances of flash flooding in Cumberland County. Flooding concerns in Lancaster County continued into the early morning hours of July 1, 2025 as the heaviest rain began to move out of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding covered the roadway with water at Split Rail Drive and Meadow Lark Lane in East Petersburg.

Locally heavy rainfall in Altoona led to multiple roadways in and around the city becoming impassable with States Road quickly being washed out by the rainfall. Later in the evening, two supercells formed across the central mountains of Pennsylvania, with the southernmost one producing large hail in Mifflin County and producing widespread tree damage in northern Huntingdon County. The northernmost supercell produced multiple trees and wires down in Milesburg. An isolated severe thunderstorm also knocked down two trees into a residence in northern Perry County, with no injuries reported. Heavy rainfall also caused isolated instances of flash flooding in central Bedford County and northern Huntingdon County, with flooding reported in Perry County during the early morning hours of June 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was reported on the roadway at the intersection of  6th Avenue and Logan Blvd in Altoona.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously strong upper-level ridge began to set up across the eastern contiguous United States, allowing for an extended period of heat across portions of Central Pennsylvania beginning on June 22, 2025. High pressure building to the south on June 23, 2025 allowed for the hottest temperatures and heat index values to occur during the afternoon hours of June 23rd and June 24th across portions of the Susquehanna Valley, highest across the southern tier of Pennsylvania. High pressure began to slowly break down later on in the day of June 24th, allowing for slightly cooling temperatures with an approaching low pressure system from the Ohio Valley allowing for further cooling by June 25, 2025.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Well-above average temperatures, coupled with humid conditions, allowed for multiple days of heat index values over 100 degrees beginning during the afternoon hours of June 22nd and continuing through the late evening hours of June 25, 2025 across Franklin County. Heat index values topped out between 105 and 107 degrees during the afternoon and evening hours of June 23, 2025. Warm and humid conditions continued into the overnight hours throughout this period, offering little relief throughout the time period. Multiple cooling stations were opened throughout the county during this stretch of abnormally cool temperatures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and humid conditions under the influence of a lee-side trough allowed for scattered thunderstorm development during the afternoon and evening hours of June 26, 2025. Ample moisture in place across the region (PWATs in 1.75 to 2.00 range), coupled with overly favorable instability (CAPE in the 2000-3000 J/kg range) allowed for pulse thunderstorms to push up towards severe limits and trend downward without sufficient shear in place across central Pennsylvania. Severe thunderstorms across south-central and southeastern Pennsylvania produced scattered instances of tree damage. This lack of shear also allowed for thunderstorms, producing efficient rainfall rates exceeding one inch per hour, to stall out and/or move slowly, prompting isolated instances of flash flooding across Lycoming County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing wind gusts estimated at 60 mph knocked trees and wires down onto Packing House Road near Pigeon Hill Road approximately 3 miles west-southwest of Spring Grove.
