EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow fell across Lancaster County from the afternoon of December 13 through the afternoon of December 14. Most of the county saw 3 to 5 inches of snow with localized amounts as high as 7 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure and shortwave trough stationed across the Midwest in the early morning hours of December 26, 2025 along with surface high pressure east of the region allowed for relatively cold surface temperatures with an elevated pocket of warm air across central Pennsylvania. As the trough moved further east into the region, freezing rain began across the Laurel Highlands, with a snow/sleet mix stationed further southeast where warmer air aloft was not as prevalent. The highest ice accumulations were reported in Cambria County, where 0.40 of ice accumulation was measured in Johnstown. Multiple accidents were reported across the Clearfield, Columbia, Clinton, and Cumberland counties with a total of four patients being transported due to injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A longer duration period of freezing rain began across Cambria County, with lower elevations experiencing freezing as early as the mid-morning hours of December 26th and continuing through the late morning hours of December 27, 2025. Higher elevations of Cambria County experienced a brief snow/freezing rain mix at onset with a transition over to freezing rain by the early afternoon hours of December 26th and continuing into the mid-morning hours of December 27th as precipitation began to taper off west-to-east across the county. John Murtha Johnstown Cambria County Airport recorded 0.40 inches of freezing rain accumulation with higher elevation locations of Cambria County reporting around a quarter of an inch of ice accumulations.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain began across lower elevations of Blair County during the mid-morning hours of December 26th and continued through early morning hours of December 27, 2025. Higher elevations of Blair County experienced a brief snow/freezing rain mix at onset with a transition over to freezing rain by the early afternoon hours of December 26th that continued into the early hours of December 27th as precipitation began to taper off west-to-east across the county. Altoona Blair County Airport recorded 0.34 inches of freezing rain accumulation with trained spotter reports north of Altoona reporting 0.30 inches of ice accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A clipper system brought widespread precipitation to Northeast PA. A warmer mid-level led to an area of mixed precipitation and even freezing rain. Wayne County received 4 to 8 inches of snow as this system moved through.  Although Northern Wayne County did not observe freezing rain, some freezing drizzle and light icing was observed in Southern Wayne and Pike Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated between 4 and 8 inches.

Multiple accidents were reported across the Clearfield, Columbia, Clinton, and Cumberland counties with a total of four patients being transported due to injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain caused slick roadways in portions of southern Clinton County, where both directions of State Route 220 was closed for most of the afternoon and evening hours of December 26th in Castanea Township due to a tractor trailer rolling over on the roadway. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper wave dropping south across the Great Lakes and it's associated cold front brought widespread accumulating snow to the region New Year's Eve and into the new year across the ridges of western Pennsylvania and northern West Virginia. Enhanced orographic lift resulted in higher totals across the Laurel Highlands and eastern West Virginia ridges, with relatively high snow ratios between 18:1 and 21:1. Widespread Advisory level snow was also observed across the region, ranging from 2.5 inches to 5.5 inches outside of the ridges of Pennsylvania and West Virginia.  A narrow, strong line of convective snow developed along the boundary sinking across the entire county warning area. There were numerous reports of visibility of a quarter mile or less, wind gusts greater than 30 mph, and rapid accumulation on roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A PennDot road sensor reported less than 999ft visibility along Interstate 80 and Interstate 79.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving clipper system tracked north of our region from early Tuesday morning through the afternoon, bringing accumulating snow primarily to the Northern Tier of Pennsylvania. The heaviest snowfall occurred between Tuesday morning and noon. While warm air aloft caused a transition to a wintry mix and light ice at higher elevations, precipitation remained mostly snow. This resulted in accumulations of 3 to 8 inches, along with a light glaze of ice in some spots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated between 4 and 8 inches.

Three patients were transported due to injuries, with the roadway remaining closed throughout the evening hours of December 26th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure stationed across Quebec allowed for an extremely tight pressure gradient to form across western Pennsylvania, with strong isallobaric forcing allowing for ample mixing of wind gusts from aloft down towards the surface beginning in the morning hours of December 29th. The strongest winds were observed during the afternoon hours when this mixing was most pronounced and the pressure gradient also allowed for strong wind gusts, before slowly tapering off into the morning hours of December 30th for much of the area; however, strong winds gusts remained into the late morning hours of December 30th across the Laurel Highlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds began during the late morning hours of December 29th and continued into the early morning hours of December 30th with the highest wind gust, measured at 60 mph, being observed at Altoona-Blair County Airport during the afternoon hours of December 29th. Gusty winds continued into the afternoon hours of December 30th before slightly diminishing by the evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent Arctic Front across southern Ontario and western New York began to shift southeasterly during the late evening hours of December 31, 2025 and continued into the early morning hours of January 1, 2026. This potent cold front allowed for a line of snow squalls to form across all of central Pennsylvania, with widespread whiteout conditions are the line of squalls moved through the area. Whiteout conditions were observed at the NWS State College office, along with by multiple spotters in Centre, Mifflin, Blair, and York counties. No accidents or injuries were reported during these snow squalls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. A snow squall brought visibility less than one quarter of a mile with heavy snow observed to cover State Route 6 east of Coudersport. Winds were observed to gust near 25 mph as the snow squall moved through the location during the late evening hours of December 31, 2025.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwesterly flow behind a departing area of low pressure allowed for lake effect snowfall across portions of northwestern Pennsylvania beginning on December 29, 2025 and continuing through January 1, 2026. Ample moisture in place from Lake Erie and continued northwesterly flow allowed for a long-duration, but light snowfall between December 29th and December 31st before heavier lake effect snow ramped up on January 1st across Warren and McKean counties. As a potent cold front moved across the area on December 31st and into January 1st, winds began to shift westerly, allowing for heavy upslope snow showers across Somerset County to allow for snowfall beginning December 31st and into January 1st. No impacts were reported with the heavy lake effect and upslope snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy lake effect snow began during the morning hours of December 29th and continued through the afternoon hours of January 1st. Light snow was mainly observed from December 29th through December 31st, with two to three inches of snowfall. Heavy snow began during the early morning hours of January 1st, mainly confined to the northern tier of Warren County, where 7.6 inches of snowfall was observed west of Russell by the end of the snowfall event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level trough and associated surface cold front with a clipper system moved across the area during the evening of Saturday, the 13th, through mid morning on Sunday, the 14th. A wave of low pressure developed along the frontal boundary just to the south of our region Saturday night and exited the mid-Atlantic coast Sunday morning. Southwesterly flow ahead of the front advected in plenty of moisture north. In combination with fresh cold air, this led to the development of snow across Northeast PA. The heaviest of snow fell Saturday night into early Sunday morning approximately from 10 PM to 4 AM EST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated between 3 and 5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving across the Ohio and Tennessee River Valleys and over the southern Appalachians transferred its energy to a surface low near the Carolina coast early on the 2nd. The coastal low strengthened as it moved northward just off the mid-Atlantic coast, drawing plenty of moisture north leading to a quick-hitting widespread snowfall during the morning and afternoon. As snow tapered off from west to east during the afternoon and early evening, it ended as a little freezing drizzle or sleet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated between 5 and 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stagnant, moist air mass just north of a stationary front resulted in areas of dense fog starting late on the 25th and continuing into the morning of the 26th. The fog dissipated after dawn.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several hours of dense fog was observed at Reading Regional Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stagnant, moist air mass just north of a stationary front resulted in areas of dense fog starting late on the 25th and continuing into the morning of the 26th. The fog dissipated after dawn.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several hours of dense fog was observed at Quakertown Airport and Pennridge Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent area of low pressure across the northern Great Lakes tracking into southern Canada allowed for enhanced westerly flow to begin during the early morning hours of November 27, 2025. This westerly flow promoted lake effect snow to occur across northwestern Pennsylvania during the early morning hours that continued throughout the day. The cold front associated with this area of low pressure tracked across the region during the morning and afternoon hours of November 28th, with ample instability and lift allowing for snow squalls to form across western Pennsylvania before moving into slightly more stable air and lowering the snow squall threat across south-central and eastern Pennsylvania. Visibilities within snow squalls were near-zero with multiple accidents observed due to icy road conditions as roadway temperatures above freezing dropped below freezing. Cold temperatures in the wake of the cold front allowed for continued icy roadways across Clearfield County, where multiple additional accidents were observed. Westerly flow in the wake of the cold front allowed for continued lake effect snow showers through the early morning hours of November 29th, when flow shifted more southwesterly, pushing lake effect snow into New York State.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. A snow squall was observed on Interstate 80 near Penfield, with temperatures dropping rapidly in heavy snow that reduced visibility below a quarter of a mile for over 20 minutes. A localized band of snowfall occurred just ahead of the squall, when road temperatures were approximately 34 degrees before falling to 27 degrees after the snow squall. After the squall occurred, multiple accidents were observed on Interstate 80 between mile markers 111 and 132. One of the accidents resulted in a minor injury to the driver of a vehicle when they lost control of their vehicle and veered off of the roadway, colliding with an embankment.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stagnant, moist air mass just north of a stationary front resulted in areas of dense fog starting late on the 25th and continuing into the morning of the 26th. The fog dissipated after dawn.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several hours of dense fog was observed at Queen City Airport.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several hours of dense fog was observed at Chester County Airport.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong wind gust was  observed at the Reading Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure stationed across New England allowed for westerly flow to generate upslope and lake effect snowfall across portions of western Pennsylvania beginning during the early morning hours of November 10th can continuing into the afternoon hours of November 11, 2025. Wind directions initially out of the west-northwest allowed for snowfall to be limited to Warren and McKean Counties during the morning hours of November 10th before flow turned westerly, allowing for more widespread lake effect snow and upslope snow across central Pennsylvania. Instability and moisture in place allowed for snow squalls to form, mainly during the evening hours of November 10th and into the November 11th across the Interstate 80 corridor. A multi-vehicle accident occurred in Clearfield County within a snow squall, where no injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. A slow moving band of lake effect snow impacted Interstate 80 in Clearfield County. The PennDOT RWIS station at mile marker 111 reported visibility below a quarter of a mile with heavy snow falling as a heavier snow squall embedded in the band moved through. Webcams showed snow accumulating on the roadway.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. A snow squall led to an accident involving three commercial vehicles on Interstate 80 at exit 97. Interstate 80 eastbound was closed for over 4 hours, resulting in a trapped queue approximately 1 mile long. No injuries were reported. The same snow squall continued along the interstate, causing a flash freeze at mile marker 120 as road temperatures fell from 33.3 degrees at 11:25 PM to 30.7 degrees at 11:55 PM according to data from the PennDOT RWIS station at exit 120.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. A snow squall impacted Interstate 99 near the NWS Office in State College where heavy snow, gusty winds, and near-zero visibilities occurred within the snow squall. A multi-vehicle pileup was observed on the northbound lane of Interstate 99 during the squall, closing the roadway for over an hour. Multiple first responders and spotters noted ice-covered roadways as the squall moved through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a strong cold front that crossed on November 26th, much colder air flowed in and allowed significant lake-effect snow to develop. This snow was initially carried into western New York by southwest winds, but gradually shifted south and west into the snowbelt region of Northwest Pennsylvania as winds turned more west-northwest on Thanksgiving. Heavy lake effect snow then continued into Black Friday before tapering off. Snow rates exceeded one inch per hour at times within lake effect bands, especially late Thanksgiving Day and night. A peak accumulation of 15.9 inches was reported in Meadville. Other select totals include 13.9 inches in Corry, 13.5 inches in Elgin Township, and 12 inches in Venango Township.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Following a strong cold front that crossed on November 26th, much colder air flowed in and allowed significant lake-effect snow to develop. This snow was initially carried into western New York by southwest winds, but gradually shifted south and west into the snowbelt region of Northwest Pennsylvania as winds turned more west-northwest on Thanksgiving. Heavy lake effect snow then continued into Black Friday before tapering off. Snow rates exceeded one inch per hour at times within lake effect bands, especially late Thanksgiving Day and night. A peak accumulation of 13.9 inches was reported by a CoCoRaHS observer in Corry. A cooperative observer in Union City reported 9 inches of snow. Selected trained spotter reports include 13.5 inches in Algin Township, 11.7 inches in Mill Village, 10 inches in Waterford and 7.6 inches in Edinboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure stationed across New England allowed for westerly flow to generate upslope and lake effect snowfall across portions of western Pennsylvania beginning during the early morning hours of November 10th can continuing into the afternoon hours of November 11, 2025. Wind directions initially out of the west-northwest allowed for snowfall to be limited to Warren and McKean Counties during the morning hours of November 10th before flow turned westerly, allowing for more widespread lake effect snow and upslope snow across central Pennsylvania. Instability and moisture in place allowed for snow squalls to form, mainly during the evening hours of November 10th and into the November 11th across the Interstate 80 corridor. A multi-vehicle accident occurred in Clearfield County within a snow squall, where no injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. A snow squall resulted in an accident involving two commercial vehicles on State Route 153 near Colby Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent area of low pressure across the northern Great Lakes tracking into southern Canada allowed for enhanced westerly flow to begin during the early morning hours of November 27, 2025. This westerly flow promoted lake effect snow to occur across northwestern Pennsylvania during the early morning hours that continued throughout the day. The cold front associated with this area of low pressure tracked across the region during the morning and afternoon hours of November 28th, with ample instability and lift allowing for snow squalls to form across western Pennsylvania before moving into slightly more stable air and lowering the snow squall threat across south-central and eastern Pennsylvania. Visibilities within snow squalls were near-zero with multiple accidents observed due to icy road conditions as roadway temperatures above freezing dropped below freezing. Cold temperatures in the wake of the cold front allowed for continued icy roadways across Clearfield County, where multiple additional accidents were observed. Westerly flow in the wake of the cold front allowed for continued lake effect snow showers through the early morning hours of November 29th, when flow shifted more southwesterly, pushing lake effect snow into New York State.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. A snow squall brought heavy snow and gusty winds, allowing for near-zero visibility to portions of Interstate 99 between State College and Port Matilda, causing a multi-vehicle incident that the northbound lane of Interstate 99. The southbound lane was later closed due to hazardous conditions associated with the snow squall, as temperatures dropped below freezing and led to icy road conditions. A trained spotter observed low visibility due to heavy snow and gusty winds with icy road conditions in the vicinity of the incident; however, no injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved across Pennsylvania on November 9th, before shifting east into New England and lingering through the 10th and 11th. Behind this low, a lingering trough and an early season blast of cold air combined with northwest winds to bring accumulating lake effect snow. The highest snow amounts occurred well-inland across parts of Crawford County. A peak accumulation of 12.1 inches was reported by a Cooperative Observer in Titusville. Trained spotters and CoCoRaHS observers reported accumulations ranging from 6 to 11 inches across much of the rest of Crawford County, with totals over 7 inches also reported in far southern Erie County in Elgin and Corry.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved across Pennsylvania on November 9th, before shifting east into New England and lingering through the 10th and 11th. Behind this low, a lingering trough and an early season blast of cold air combined with northwest winds to bring accumulating lake effect snow. The snow was spread out over a prolonged period of time (about 36 hours) and did not hit local heavy snow criteria. However, total accumulations as high as 8.5 inches were reported on social media by a ski resort in Edinboro. Other selected spotter reports include 7.8 inches in Elgin Township and 6.3 inches Greenfield Township, with a CoCoRaHS observer in Corry reported a storm total snow accumulation of 7.2 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. A snow squall impacted Route 22 across all of Cambria County, bringing heavy snowfall and gusty winds that allowed for near-zero visibility. The roadway was closed for multiple hours as a result of this incident and the resultant icy roadways.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A persistent lake effect snow band developed over the northern half of Clearfield County on the evening of Monday, November 10, 2025 and continued into the early afternoon on Tuesday, November 11, 2025. Many areas saw 2 to 4 inches of snow as a result of this band. The highest snowfall total in the county was 5.5 inches in Grampian. Two tractor trailers jack knifed on State Route 153 in Huston Township, closing the roadway between the Interstate 80 westbound ramp. No injuries were reported from these incidents.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Icy road conditions from earlier snow squalls coupled with multiple transient snow showers allowed for continued wintry driving conditions across Clearfield County throughout the evening hours of November 28. Multiple incidents were reported on Interstate 80 and State Route 879 were reported throughout the afternoon and early evening hours with both roadways being closed for multiple hours before opening. The multi-vehicle accident on Interstate 80 caused minor injuries to a motorist with another being transported for treatment.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: West-northwesterly flow in the wake of a potent cold front stationed offshore the Mid-Atlantic allowed for lake effect snow showers and squalls to form during the morning hours of November 17, 2025. Ample moisture in place, coupled with lake induced instability over relatively warm waters on Lake Erie allowed for a band of snow squalls to form northwest of the Interstate 80 corridor. Steering flow pushed squalls over Interstate 80 in Clearfield and Centre Counties, where an initial snow squall was observed in the early morning hours before a longer duration snow squall was observed. Road temperatures just above freezing ahead of the squall allowed for flash freeze conditions to occur with road surface temperatures dropping 2 to 4 degrees within the squall. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. A snow squall moved across Interstate 80 in Clearfield County on the morning of November 17, 2025. The PennDOT RWIS station at mile marker 111 reported visibility below one quarter of a mile in heavy snow as the squall moved through and the road surface temperature dropped from 31.3 degrees to 28.2 degrees in 15 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a cold front moved through during the evening hours of the 5th, a line of gusty showers and isolated thunderstorms swept across Northeast Pennsylvania. A couple of embedded storms became severe as very high wind shear and steep temperature lapse rates made conditions unstable and favorable for damaging wind gusts to mix down to the surface. Numerous trees were knocked down from the damaging winds that occurred mainly behind the convective line, along with a few wires. A tree also fell onto and damaged a propane tank.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree onto a road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought across north-central Pennsylvania towards the tail end of October continued into the early stages of November, with beneficial rainfall during the last week of October and early November allowing for improvement in drought conditions. Severe drought was removed from north-central Pennsylvania on November 4, 2025.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Due to abnormally dry conditions September into October, severe (D2) drought continued across all of McKean County through November 4, 2025. A widespread 1.5-2.5 of rainfall on October 30, 2025 helped bring an end to D2 conditions across McKean County with the USDM update on November 4, 2025.

Visibility fell to approximately one third of a mile as the squall moved through.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the wake of a cold front, northwesterly flow led to the development of lake effect snow showers in the area. A snow shower dropped south across southern Luzerne County and I-81 early in the morning on the 17th. This snow shower briefly dropped road temperatures below freezing and the snow that melted initially on contact created black ice according to the local fire chief. This led to a multi-vehicle accident involving 8 vehicles around 6 AM EST on I-81 southbound between West Hazleton and the 309 McAdoo-Tamaqua exit. Multiple injuries were reported and unfortunately one of the drivers involved passed away on scene.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A multi-vehicle accident occurred on I-81 southbound in the wake of a snow shower that went through during the early morning hours. The likely cause according to a local fire chief was black ice. Multiple injuries were reported, but there was no confirmation of the actual number of individuals. However, there was one confirmed fatality on scene.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening area of low pressure stationed across the Upper Midwest and into the Great Lakes allowed for a tightening pressure gradient with an associated cold frontal passage west-to-east across central Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening hours of November 5, 2025. Sufficient moisture and lift ahead of the cold frontal passage allowed for a convective line of gusty showers during the afternoon hours, with severe winds observed at Clearfield Lawrence Airport. The cold frontal passage across central Pennsylvania came later in the evening, with another severe gust observed at Clearfield Lawrence Airport with isolated instances of tree damage observed across the Lower Susquehanna Valley. A severe wind gust was also observed at Harrisburg International Airport as the line of showers moved through the region. Gusty winds continued after the cold frontal passage, which caused isolated wind damage in Dauphin County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm, producing wind gusts estimated near 60 mph, knocked down a tree onto wires in Dubois.

Gusty winds continued after the cold frontal passage, which caused isolated wind damage in Dauphin County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm, producing wind gusts estimated near 60 mph, knocked a tree down that fell into the garage of a residence where it landed on the car of the resident southwest of East Petersburg. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent area of low pressure across the northern Great Lakes tracking into southern Canada allowed for enhanced westerly flow to begin during the early morning hours of November 27, 2025. This westerly flow promoted lake effect snow to occur across northwestern Pennsylvania during the early morning hours that continued throughout the day. The cold front associated with this area of low pressure tracked across the region during the morning and afternoon hours of November 28th, with ample instability and lift allowing for snow squalls to form across western Pennsylvania before moving into slightly more stable air and lowering the snow squall threat across south-central and eastern Pennsylvania. Visibilities within snow squalls were near-zero with multiple accidents observed due to icy road conditions as roadway temperatures above freezing dropped below freezing. Cold temperatures in the wake of the cold front allowed for continued icy roadways across Clearfield County, where multiple additional accidents were observed. Westerly flow in the wake of the cold front allowed for continued lake effect snow showers through the early morning hours of November 29th, when flow shifted more southwesterly, pushing lake effect snow into New York State.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy lake effect snow began across northern Warren County during the morning hours of November 27th and continued into the early morning hours of November 29, 2025. The heaviest snowfall across the region was observed mainly during the evening hours of November 27th and through the morning hours of November 28th, where upwards of 11 inches of snowfall was observed by multiple CoCoRaHS stations near Russell. A couple of lake effect snow bands continued across the county throughout the afternoon and evening hours of November 28th before light snow began to taper off during the overnight hours into November 29th, adding two inches of snowfall. Multiple snowfall reports in Warren County exceeding one foot with 13.5 inches of snowfall observed 4.7 miles west of Russell, 13.4 inches of snowfall observed 0.7 miles south of Russell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface cold front stationed across western Pennsylvania during the morning hours of September 25, 2025 moved eastward across the Commonwealth throughout the afternoon and evening hours. While instability remained limited due to expansive cloud coverage ahead of the frontal passage, robust lift and ample shear allowed for thunderstorm development to occur across south-central Pennsylvania with strong-to-severe thunderstorms developing and tracking into southeastern Pennsylvania. Isolated instances of tree damage were observed in Adams and York counties from a severe thunderstorm, with all other thunderstorms remaining below severe limits as the cold front cleared central Pennsylvania during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm, producing wind gusts estimated near 60 mph, knocked down a tree onto Mountain Road between Pine Tree Road and State Route 234 near Michaux State Forest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm accompanied by strong warm advection aloft brought a widespread wintry mix of precipitation to portions of the region. A coating of snow and sleet, as well as a coating of freezing rain was reported across much of eastern Pennsylvania, New Jersey, and northern Delmarva. The heaviest totals were reported across the Lehigh Valley and southern Poconos, where 1-2 of snow and sleet fell. Ice amounts were slightly greater farther south toward the Philadelphia metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and sleet totals ranged from 1-2. The highest report was 1.9 from Canadensis. A light glaze of freezing rain/freezing drizzle also occurred in some areas across the county. As much as 0.02 of ice was reported at the Pocono Mountains Municipal Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ample moisture and a near-stationary boundary assisted in the development of slow moving thunderstorms late on June 14th. Precipitable water values exceeding 90% of climatology, which supported efficient rainfall. Catastrophic flash flooding occurred in portions of Wheeling WV and that complex of storms expanded eastward, dumping 2-3 inches in an hour over portions of Washington county as well. There were several reports of stranded vehicles and flooding around an into some homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported numerous roads flooded around North Franklin  township. Creeks out of their banks. 2.00 inches of rain in less than an hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface cold front stationed across west-central Pennsylvania produced scattered thunderstorms into central and eastern Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening hours of September 4, 2025. Thunderstorms remained strong-to-severe across north-central Pennsylvania, producing isolated wind damage. Shear in the lowest levels allowed for a brief, weak tornado to form in Lancaster County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm, producing wind gusts estimated near 60 mph, knocked down wires onto Buckhorn Road near the intersection of Schoolhouse Road in Buckhorn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface cold front stationed across west-central Pennsylvania produced scattered thunderstorms into central and eastern Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening hours of September 4, 2025. Thunderstorms remained strong-to-severe across north-central Pennsylvania, producing isolated wind damage. Shear in the lowest levels allowed for a brief, weak tornado to form in Lancaster County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-0 tornado with an estimated peak wind of 70 mph occurred southeast of Collins, PA  on the evening of Thursday, September 4, 2025. It lifted the roof off of a dugout at the Solanco Youth Baseball Association and then continued across Kirkwood Pike, where it caused minor crop damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm, moist airmass remained in place through the latter half of the month which allowed for daily shower and thunderstorm chances. Training storms was the primary factor to the resultant flash flooding as cells developed upshear of the initial updrafts in West Virginia, Ohio, and Pennsylvania. One of these storms did result in yet another round of flash flooding for portions of Wheeling WV.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported a few streets were flooded and impassable in Donora.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wintry weather system brought light freezing rain to portions of the region, resulting in a light glaze of ice in portions of northern New Jersey and eastern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on CoCoRaHS reports and automated stations, a few hundredths of an inch of ice accretion occurred. The highest liquid precipitation amount was 0.16, with 0.10 of ice reported in Effort.

A warm, moist airmass remained in place through the latter half of the month which allowed for daily shower and thunderstorm chances. Training storms was the primary factor to the resultant flash flooding as cells developed upshear of the initial updrafts in West Virginia, Ohio, and Pennsylvania. One of these storms did result in yet another round of flash flooding for portions of Wheeling WV.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe water rescues from vehicles along Campbells Run Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wintry system brought widespread moderate rainfall to the region, and the first accumulating snowfall, sleet, and ice of the season to portions of northern New Jersey and eastern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light to moderate snowfall, sleet, and a light glaze of ice occurred. Snowfall totals ranged from 3-5. The highest snowfall report was 5.4 from Canadensis. 0.03 of ice was reported at the Pocono Mountains Municipal Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wintry system brought widespread moderate rainfall to the region, and the first accumulating snowfall, sleet, and ice of the season to portions of northern New Jersey and eastern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light to moderate snowfall, sleet, and a light glaze of ice occurred. Snowfall totals ranged from 3-5. The highest snowfall report was 5.1 from Lake Harmony. No ice measurement reports were received, but surrounding reports indicate that at least trace amounts of ice likely occurred.
